syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-20-50-ce.bio: Woman of Oxyrhynchus who married Sarapion, bringing a dowry of 200 silver drachmae and receiving him into her parents' house. After Sarapion squandered her dowry, abused her physically and verbally, deprived her of necessities, and deserted her, she petitioned the archidikastes Heraclides for return of her dowry with 50% penalty. Appears to have acted without a male guardian.
syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-20-50-ce.disambiguation: Not otherwise attested. The name Syra ("Syrian") was a conventional ethnic name that did not necessarily indicate Syrian origin.

sarapion-husband-of-syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-.bio: Husband of Syra, described as utterly destitute at the time of marriage. Was received into Syra's parents' house. Squandered her 200-drachma dowry, abused her, and deserted her. No patronymic survives.
sarapion-husband-of-syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-.disambiguation: Distinct from Sarapion son of Theon the weaver (P.Oxy. 2.285). One of numerous men named Sarapion in Oxyrhynchus.

theon-father-of-syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-1st-.bio: Father of Syra. Owned a house in Oxyrhynchus in which Syra's husband Sarapion was received. Otherwise unattested.
theon-father-of-syra-oxyrhynchus-fl-1st-.disambiguation: Not to be confused with the archidikastes Theon (P.Oxy. 2.268), Theon the groom in P.Oxy. 3.497, or Theon son of Demetrius (P.Oxy. 2.347). Possibly father of Sarapion son of Theon the weaver (P.Oxy. 2.285).

heraclides-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-2.bio: Priest and chief justice (archidikastes), superintendent of the chrematistae and other courts. Based in Alexandria. Addressee of Syra's petition.
heraclides-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-2.disambiguation: Distinct from Pallas (81 CE), Theon (after 57 CE), and Aurelius Gaius (267 CE), who held the same office at later dates.

sarapion-son-of-theon-weaver-oxyrhynchus.bio: Weaver of Oxyrhynchus living in the Gymnasium Square quarter. Petitioned about extortion by Apollophanes, who seized his tunic and extorted 24 drachmae over six months. Possibly Syra's brother.
sarapion-son-of-theon-weaver-oxyrhynchus.disambiguation: Distinct from Sarapion the husband of Syra. The shared patronymic with Syra is suggestive but not conclusive.

name_variants: Tryphon son of Dionysius son of Tryphon
tryphon-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-ca-.bio: One of the best-documented individuals from 1st-century Oxyrhynchus. Weaver designated "Persian of the Epigone." Married twice. Released from service in 52 CE due to cataract. Purchased a loom in 54 CE. Apprenticed his son in 66 CE. His archive provides the closest parallel to Syra's world.
tryphon-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-ca-.disambiguation: "Persian of the Epigone" was a purely bureaucratic ethnic designation.

thoönis-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-qua.bio: Son of Tryphon by Demetrous. Had no trade and no means. Reported as having "removed abroad" by his grandmother in 44 CE.
thoönis-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-qua.disambiguation: Distinct from Thoenis son of Tryphon (by Saraeus), apprenticed in 66 CE.

thoenis-son-of-tryphon-oxyrhynchus-born-.bio: Son of Tryphon and Saraeus. Apprenticed to the weaver Ptolemaeus for one year in 66 CE.
thoenis-son-of-tryphon-oxyrhynchus-born-.disambiguation: Distinct from Thoönis son of Dionysius (Tryphon's son by Demetrous).

pallas-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-81-ce.bio: Priest and archidikastes serving in 81 CE. Handled loan registrations with hemiolion penalty clauses.
pallas-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-81-ce.disambiguation: Distinct from Heraclides (20-50 CE) and Theon (after 57 CE).

theon-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-after-.bio: Chief justice serving after 57 CE. Handled a dowry settlement of 800 drachmae involving Oxyrhynchus families.
theon-archidikastes-alexandria-fl-after-.disambiguation: Not the same Theon as Syra's father.

sarapion-correspondent-oxyrhynchus-alexa.bio: Man who wrote from Alexandria to his brother Dorion, mentioning house searches, pressure to enter service, and asking to look after Demetrous and their father.
sarapion-correspondent-oxyrhynchus-alexa.disambiguation: Not identifiable with Syra's husband or Sarapion son of Theon the weaver.

dionysia-oxyrhynchus-c-105--after-186-ce.patronymic: daughter of Chaeremon son of Phanias
dionysia-oxyrhynchus-c-105--after-186-ce.bio: One of the most fully documented women from Roman Egypt. Across at least sixty years (126--186 CE), she purchased and managed agricultural and urban property, survived a catastrophic Nile flood, and fought two major legal battles -- first against a man who challenged her vineyard purchase, then against her own father who tried to reclaim her property and invoke an ancient Egyptian law to remove her from her husband's house. Her petition P.Oxy. 2.237 is one of the longest and most legally sophisticated papyri ever discovered.
dionysia-oxyrhynchus-c-105--after-186-ce.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Dionysia daughter of Theon (P.Oxy. 3.494, corpus ID 141058, 165 CE) or Aurelia Dionysias alias Chaeremonis (P.Oxy. 10.1278, corpus ID 43204, 214 CE).

chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-160--186-ce.bio: Dionysia's father, an ex-gymnasiarch of Oxyrhynchus -- the highest civic magistracy, requiring substantial personal wealth. He waged a decades-long campaign to reclaim property he had given to Dionysia and to invoke the ancient Egyptian law of apospasis (forcible removal of a daughter from her husband). Every official who investigated his claims found against him.
chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-160--186-ce.disambiguation: Not the same as Chaeremon of Antinoë, Dionysia's second cousin, who previously owned the house she leased in 164 CE.

hermione-oxyrhynchus-d-c-130--131-ce.bio: Dionysia's mother, who co-managed property with her daughter. Accused of pharmakeia (poisoning) by Sarapion son of Mnesitheus as a tactic in his property dispute. Died before the case could come to trial.

horion-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-160--186-ce.bio: Dionysia's husband, whom Chaeremon blamed for instigating his daughter's resistance. Chaeremon accused Horion of threatening violence and claimed Dionysia acted at his instigation.

apion-oxyrhynchus-fl-142--164-ce.bio: Dionysia's son, who served as her legal guardian (kyrios) in property transactions from at least 142 to 164 CE. By 164 CE he held the priesthood of the deified empress Faustina Augusta, confirming the family's elite status.
apion-oxyrhynchus-fl-142--164-ce.disambiguation: Not the same as Apion, father of Horion (Dionysia's father-in-law), though the naming pattern suggests the grandson was named after the paternal grandfather.

mnesitheus-oxyrhynchus-d-before-c-127-ce.bio: The man who sold vineyard and corn-land to Dionysia around 126/7 CE. He had creditors who held the land as mortgage security, suggesting financial difficulties. His death triggered his son Sarapion's challenge to the sale.
mnesitheus-oxyrhynchus-d-before-c-127-ce.disambiguation: Possibly related to the Mnesitheus son of Theon attested in Stud.Pal. 4, pp. 114--5 (corpus ID 141428).

sarapion-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-127--131-ce.bio: Son of Mnesitheus, who sold vineyard and corn-land to Dionysia. After his father's death, Sarapion challenged the sale, claiming the land was merely mortgaged. He also accused Dionysia's mother Hermione of poisoning. He was ordered to appear before the praefect in Alexandria but repeatedly failed to attend.

titus-flavius-titianus-alexandria-fl-126.bio: Praefect of Egypt under Hadrian, to whom Dionysia petitioned in 131 CE about the vineyard dispute. His ruling in a separate case -- that a wife's own choice determined where she lived -- became the foundational legal precedent that Dionysia cited fifty-five years later against her father.

roles.title: epistrategus of the Heptanomis and Arsinoite nome

julius-varianus-heptanomis-fl-131-ce.bio: Epistrategus to whom the praefect Titianus referred Dionysia's vineyard case for local adjudication.

claudius-quintianus-heptanomis-fl-c-128-.bio: Epistrategus who first heard the combined property dispute and poisoning accusation between Dionysia/Hermione and Sarapion. He referred the case to the praefect in Alexandria.

pomponius-faustianus-alexandria-fl-c-185.bio: Praefect of Egypt to whom Dionysia addressed her massive petition against her father Chaeremon in 186 CE. His predecessor Longaeus Rufus had already investigated and found in Dionysia's favor.

longaeus-rufus-alexandria-fl-c-185-ce.bio: Praefect of Egypt who investigated Chaeremon's initial claims and ordered the strategus to examine Dionysia's case. The investigation confirmed Dionysia's property rights.

harpocration-oxyrhynchus-fl-186-ce.bio: Royal scribe and acting deputy-strategus who presided over the hearing on June 3, 186 CE. He declined to rule on Chaeremon's claim of apospasis and referred the matter to the praefect for a definitive decision.

isidorus-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-185--186-ce.bio: Strategus (nome governor) who carried out the praefect's instructions to investigate Chaeremon's claims and ordered the keepers of the archives to produce their reports.

ulpius-dionysodorus-oxyrhynchus-fl-c-185.bio: Legal counsel retained by Dionysia. His formal opinion -- that 'since Dionysia has been given away by her father in marriage, she is no longer in his power' -- was a key element of her legal argument against the right of apospasis.

paconius-felix-heptanomis-fl-1334-ce.bio: Epistrategus who ruled in the case of Phlauesis vs. Heron, following the precedent of Flavius Titianus that the wife's own choice determined where she lived.

umbrius-alexandria-fl-867-ce.bio: Roman judicial official who ruled that 'it is worse to take away a wife from her husband than a dowry from a daughter' -- a precedent cited by Dionysia in her petition a century later.

marcus-mettius-rufus-alexandria-fl-8990-.bio: Praefect of Egypt who issued the proclamation establishing the bibliotheke enkteseon (property registration system) in 89/90 CE. His decree required all property owners to register their holdings and wives to insert marriage contracts in husbands' property statements. This system was the foundation of Dionysia's legal victories.

psenamounis-paberke-fl-142-ce.bio: Tenant farmer from Paberke in the eastern toparchy, described as 'a Persian of the Epigone.' He leased 38 arourae from Dionysia for six years beginning in 142 CE at a rent of 190 artabae of wheat and 12 drachmas per year.

ptolema-antinoë-fl-164-ce.bio: Woman from Antinoë who leased a house from Dionysia in Oxyrhynchus in 164 CE, acting through an agent named Iulas son of Didymus. The annual rent was 200 silver drachmas.

valerius-eudaemon-alexandria-fl-1378-ce.bio: Praefect who issued a decree against debtors bringing criminal counter-accusations to avoid payment -- the same tactic Sarapion used against Dionysia and Hermione.

phlauesis-unknown-fl-1334-ce.bio: Father who tried to reclaim his daughter Taeichekis from her husband Heron, citing the Egyptian law of apospasis. The epistrategus Paconius Felix ruled that the daughter's own choice determined the matter.

taeichekis-unknown-fl-1334-ce.bio: Daughter of Phlauesis, who chose to remain with her husband Heron when the epistrategus ruled that her choice controlled the question of apospasis.

aurelius-heroneinos-theadelphia-arsinoit.bio: Estate manager (phrontistes) who ran the Theadelphia division of the Appianus estate in the Fayum for at least 19 years during the Crisis of the Third Century. Approximately 247 documents chronicle his wine production, grain transport, labor management, and financial accounting. His son Maximos served as diastoleus for the estate.
aurelius-heroneinos-theadelphia-arsinoit.disambiguation: Not to be confused with an earlier Heroneinos at Theadelphia attested in P.Freib. 4.70 (206 CE), probably a grandfather or great-uncle.

aurelius-appianus-alexandria-fl-249-268-.bio: Wealthy Alexandrian landowner whose vast estate in the Fayum was managed by village-level phrontistai. His letters reveal an imperious, demanding personality given to threats over trivial matters like jujubes and figs, but also capable of generosity toward former household servants. Brothers: Ophellios and Apollonios.

alypios-arsinoe-nome-capital-fl-257-267-.bio: Head administrator of the Appianus estate, based at the nome capital of Arsinoe. Coordinated between Appianus in Alexandria and the village managers. Efficient and businesslike but memorable for his complaint about a thin piglet. Assistant: Ischyrion.

aurelius-maximos-theadelphia-alexandria-.bio: Son of Heroneinos the phrontistes. Served as diastoleus for the Appianus estate, managing wine shipments to the city. Addressed Heroneinos as "father" in November 264 CE and acknowledged receipt of over 1,900 jars of wine.

ischyrion-arsinoe-fl-249-ce.bio: Secretary and assistant to the chief administrator Alypios. Frequently relayed orders to Heroneinos.

syros-arsinoe-or-sathro-fl-255-257-ce.bio: Mid-level administrator who coordinated between estates. Wrote to Heroneinos about the children of Pontikos and reported on canal maintenance needs.

pontikos-theadelphia-fl-251-255-ce.bio: Served as Heroneinos's assistant at Theadelphia, earning 20 drachmas per month. Disappears from the wage lists by the mid-250s. A letter of October 255 from Syros concerns his children, suggesting Pontikos had died or become incapacitated.

sodike-theadelphia-fl-254-258-ce.bio: Female shepherdess at Theadelphia who appears on transport ostraca between 254 and 258 CE, carrying grain by donkey. One of the very few women visible in the operational records of the Appianus estate.

aurelius-euprodokios-theadelphia-fl-250-.bio: Slave of Aurelius Appianus who submitted a sacrifice certificate (libellus) during the Decian persecution of 250 CE.

aurelius-sarapammon-servant-theadelphia-.bio: Servant of Appianus who submitted a Decian sacrifice certificate at Theadelphia in 250 CE.
aurelius-sarapammon-servant-theadelphia-.disambiguation: Not to be confused with the worker Sarapammon listed in P.Flor. 3.321 at 4 drachmas/month, nor with the overseer Sarapammon who directed vintage operations.

eirenaios-euhemeria-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Manager (phrontistes) of the Euhemeria division of the Appianus estate.

palas-philoteris-fl-265-ce.bio: Manager (phrontistes) of the Philoteris division of the Appianus estate. Shipped 80 Oxyrhynchite jars in the October 265 register.

horion-dionysias-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Manager (phrontistes) of the Dionysias and Sentrepaei divisions.

hatres-theadelphia-fl-254-260-ce.bio: Cowherd at Theadelphia earning 48 drachmas per month.

dorion-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.bio: Ox-driver at Theadelphia earning 48 drachmas per month.

kastor-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.bio: Senior donkey-driver at Theadelphia earning 17 drachmas 1 obol per month.

pouaris-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.bio: Donkey-driver at Theadelphia earning 8 drachmas per month.

kiot-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.bio: Foreman at Theadelphia earning 8 drachmas per month plus 1 artaba wheat.

hermes-theadelphia-fl-252-255-ce.bio: Household slave at Theadelphia earning 4 drachmas per month. Also sent to guard palm trees.

monimos-fl-257-ce.bio: Received wine shipments and complained about receiving vinegar.

herais-theadelphia-or-alexandria-fl-258-.bio: Former wet-nurse of the Appianus household who received a charitable gift of two jars of wine in February 258 CE.

ophellios-alexandria-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Brother of Aurelius Appianus, involved in estate management.

apollonios-fl-255-ce.bio: Brother of Appianus who cut timber in the fields, requiring Heroneinos's bull-teams to drag it before the flood.

ninnos-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Agent handling wine transport and inter-estate coordination.

sarapammon-overseer-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Estate overseer who directed vintage operations.
sarapammon-overseer-fl-249-268-ce.disambiguation: Distinct from Aurelius Sarapammon the servant who filed a Decian libellus, and from the worker Sarapammon in P.Flor. 3.321.

deios-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.bio: Worker at Theadelphia appearing in multiple roles. One Deios served as cowherd at varying wages; another as donkey-guard. These may be two different individuals sharing a common name.
deios-theadelphia-fl-252-260-ce.disambiguation: Possibly two individuals: Deios the cowherd and Deios the donkey-guard.

polion-theadelphia-fl-252-ce.bio: Cowherd at Theadelphia earning 17 drachmas 1 obol per month.

patas-theadelphia-fl-254-260-ce.bio: Ox-driver at Theadelphia earning 34 drachmas 2 obols per month.

aseis-theadelphia-fl-252-ce.bio: Cowherd at Theadelphia earning 48 drachmas per month.
aseis-theadelphia-fl-252-ce.disambiguation: The names Aseis, Akies, and Abies may represent the same individual or distinct workers; the papyri are ambiguous.

name_variants: Techosis daughter of Diodorus and Techosis
aurelia-techosis-small-oasis-bahariya-ox.bio: Woman originally from the Small Oasis (Bahariya Oasis), residing in Oxyrhynchus by 295 CE. She petitioned the beneficiarius Aurelius Ammonius on 8 February 295 CE after her mother's death, claiming intestate inheritance and reporting that two neighbours had robbed her mother's quarters of gold, furniture, and valuable clothes while she was occupied with funeral arrangements. She was illiterate and had her petition written by the scribe Aurelius Agathodaemon son of Serenus. Her petition reveals a sophisticated two-stage legal strategy: requesting security bonds from the accused and announcing her intention to escalate the case to the praefect of Egypt.
aurelia-techosis-small-oasis-bahariya-ox.disambiguation: Not to be confused with her mother, also named Techosis. The daughter bears the nomen Aurelia, indicating Roman citizenship under the Constitutio Antoniniana.

techosis-mother-small-oasis-bahariya-oxy.bio: Mother of the petitioner Aurelia Techosis and wife of Diodorus. Originally from the Small Oasis, she died intestate in Oxyrhynchus shortly before 8 February 295 CE after an illness during which her daughter nursed her. She had accumulated considerable property including gold, furniture, and valuable clothes, all of which were stolen by neighbours after her death.
techosis-mother-small-oasis-bahariya-oxy.disambiguation: Mother of the petitioner, who bore the same name -- a common Egyptian practice of matronymic naming.

diodorus-small-oasis-bahariya-or-oxyrhyn.bio: Father of Aurelia Techosis. Named in the petition's identification formula but absent from the narrative of illness, death, and robbery. His Greek name (meaning "gift of Zeus") provides no definitive evidence of ethnicity or origin. He may have died before 295 CE or may have remained in the Small Oasis.
diodorus-small-oasis-bahariya-or-oxyrhyn.disambiguation: Common Greek name in Roman Egypt. This Diodorus is identified only through his daughter's petition, P.Oxy. 8.1121.

roles.title: beneficiarius of the praefect of Egypt

aurelius-ammonius-oxyrhynchus-fl-295-ce.bio: Military officer holding the rank of beneficiarius, stationed at a fixed post (statio) in Oxyrhynchus and attached to the praefect of Egypt. He received Aurelia Techosis's petition on 8 February 295 CE. Filing notes on the verso indicate he was simultaneously handling at least one other petition, from a woman named Tasabes against her children. His Egyptian-derived name (from the god Ammon) combined with the nomen Aurelius suggests an Egyptian-born Roman citizen in military service.
aurelius-ammonius-oxyrhynchus-fl-295-ce.disambiguation: Identified as beneficiarius praefecti Aegypti in P.Oxy. 8.1121.

sotas-oxyrhynchus-fl-295-ce.bio: Neighbour of Techosis's mother in the same multi-unit house in Oxyrhynchus. Accused by Aurelia Techosis of carrying off her deceased mother's gold, furniture, clothes, and other movable property while Techosis was occupied with funeral arrangements.

papontos-oxyrhynchus-fl-295-ce.bio: Neighbour of Techosis's mother in the same multi-unit house in Oxyrhynchus. Together with Sotas, accused by Aurelia Techosis of stealing her mother's property after the mother's death.

aurelius-agathodaemon-oxyrhynchus-fl-295.bio: Professional scribe who wrote Aurelia Techosis's petition on her behalf because she was illiterate. His role likely extended beyond mere transcription to informal legal advising -- the petition's rhetorical sophistication and precise legal terminology suggest a scribe familiar with petition formulae and Roman legal concepts.
aurelius-agathodaemon-oxyrhynchus-fl-295.disambiguation: Identified as the writer of P.Oxy. 8.1121.

tasabes-oxyrhynchus-probable-fl-295-ce.bio: Woman who filed a petition with the same beneficiarius Aurelius Ammonius, apparently complaining against her own children. Known only from a filing note on the verso of P.Oxy. 8.1121.
tasabes-oxyrhynchus-probable-fl-295-ce.disambiguation: Known only from a verso note on P.Oxy. 8.1121.

polydeukes-unknown-fl-295-ce-probable.bio: Person named in a damaged filing note on the verso of P.Oxy. 8.1121: "To Polydeukes from the [...] before this year's time." The context is unclear.
polydeukes-unknown-fl-295-ce-probable.disambiguation: Known only from a fragmentary verso note on P.Oxy. 8.1121.

aurelius-gordianos-karanis-region-fl-297.bio: Beneficiarius on station duty who received a petition from Aurelia Taesis daughter of Kopres about violence, dated after 20 November 297 CE. His case parallels Techosis's petition two years earlier.

aurelia-taesis-karanis-fl-297-ce.bio: Woman from the village of Karanis who petitioned beneficiarius Aurelius Gordianos about violence and an unlawful attack, using language closely parallel to Techosis's petition.

valerius-pompeianus-alexandria-seat-of-p.bio: Praefect of Egypt who issued proclamations affecting both the Oxyrhynchite nome and the Small Oasis, including a decree on orphan guardianship (287 CE) and instructions to the strategi of the Heptanomia and Small Oasis (289 CE).
valerius-pompeianus-alexandria-seat-of-p.disambiguation: Praefect of Egypt attested ca. 287-289 CE.

aurelius-plutarchus-oxyrhynchus-small-oa.bio: Official sent to the Small Oasis in 299 CE for the discharge of soldiers stationed there, by order of the praefect Aelius Publius. His petition reveals active military connections between Oxyrhynchus and the Small Oasis in the same decade as Techosis's petition.

aurelia-tinoutis-unknown-fl-269-ce.bio: Woman who formally renounced her deceased brother's intestate estate in 269 CE to avoid liability for his debts.
aurelia-tinoutis-unknown-fl-269-ce.disambiguation: Also called Theodora; attested in P.Ryl. 2.117.

clodius-culcianus-alexandria-seat-of-pra.bio: Praefect of Egypt who enforced Diocletian's Great Persecution against Christians beginning in 303 CE. His praefecture began approximately eight years after Techosis filed her petition.

euphrosyne-oxyrhynchus-ca-51---after-86-.bio: House-born slave freed at age 35 on 16 October 86 CE by her mistress Aloine daughter of Komon. Born in the household ca. 51 CE, daughter of the slave Demetrous. Manumitted under the Zeus-Earth-Sun formula by ransom through the agoranomus of Oxyrhynchus. Her name means "Joy" and is shared with one of the Three Graces of Greek mythology. She disappears from the record after this single attestation.
euphrosyne-oxyrhynchus-ca-51---after-86-.disambiguation: Distinct from Euphrosynus (male) in P.Oxy. 12.1451 (corpus ID 69362), a house-born slave of the Roman citizen Trunnia, 175 CE.

demetrous-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.bio: Slave in the household of Aloine daughter of Komon in Oxyrhynchus. Mother of Euphrosyne. Her name derives from Demeter, goddess of grain. No further information survives; she cannot be definitively identified with any of the other women named Demetrous in the Oxyrhynchus papyri.
demetrous-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.disambiguation: Not the Demetrous daughter of Ammonius in P.Oxy. 2.346 (corpus ID 43427), who was a free woman.

aloine-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.patronymic: daughter of Komon son of Dionysius
aloine-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.bio: Free woman of Oxyrhynchus who manumitted her house-born slave Euphrosyne on 16 October 86 CE. Daughter of Komon son of Dionysius. Required a male guardian (kyrios) for the transaction; her full brother Dioscorus had died, so guardianship passed to Dioscorus's son, also named Komon. No husband is named in the document.
aloine-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.disambiguation: No other attestation of this name in the Oxyrhynchus papyri.

komon-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.bio: Father of Aloine (the slave-owner) and Dioscorus. Son of Dionysius. Named in the manumission document as part of Aloine's genealogical identification. Whether alive at the time of the manumission is unclear.
komon-son-of-dionysius-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.disambiguation: Distinct from his grandson Komon son of Dioscorus, who served as Aloine's guardian.

dioscorus-son-of-komon-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.patronymic: son of Komon son of Dionysius
dioscorus-son-of-komon-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.bio: Full brother (gnesios adelphos) of Aloine, son of Komon son of Dionysius. Deceased by the time of Euphrosyne's manumission in 86 CE. His son Komon served as Aloine's legal guardian in the transaction.
dioscorus-son-of-komon-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.disambiguation: Not the freedman Dioscorus in P.Oxy. 10.1263 (corpus ID 43194).

komon-son-of-dioscorus-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.patronymic: son of Dioscorus son of Komon
komon-son-of-dioscorus-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.bio: Son of the deceased Dioscorus, nephew of Aloine. Named after his grandfather Komon son of Dionysius, following the alternating-generation naming convention (papponymy). Served as Aloine's legal guardian (kyrios) for the manumission of Euphrosyne.
komon-son-of-dioscorus-oxyrhynchus-1st-c.disambiguation: Distinct from his grandfather Komon son of Dionysius.

chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.bio: Wrote to the agoranomus of Oxyrhynchus to authorize Euphrosyne's manumission. Also appears (possibly the same individual) in P.Oxy. 75.5051, writing to the agoranomus to register the sale of the slave Plousia, using an identical format. May have been a nominee or deputy of the agoranomos's office. His name means "rejoice" -- the same verb (chairein) that opens his letter.
chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.disambiguation: Possibly identical with the Chaeremon in P.Oxy. 75.5051 (corpus ID 44088). Distinct from Chaeremon son of Chaeremon of the Maronian deme in P.Oxy. 2.243 (corpus ID 141037), and from Chaeremon son of Onnophris in P.Oxy. 7.1028 (corpus ID 44043).

apollonous-oxyrhynchus-ca-65---after-91-.bio: Slave freed in Oxyrhynchus on 29 August 91 CE, five years after Euphrosyne. Aged about 26. Freed under the Zeus-Earth-Sun formula by ransom. Had been freed in stages: the other two-thirds of her ownership had already been liberated. Ransom paid by Heraclas son of Tryphon. Physical description: middle height, fair, long face, scar on the right foot.

achilleus-oxyrhynchus-ca-71---after-91-c.bio: Brother of Sarapas. Aged about 20 in 91 CE. Co-freed the slave Apollonous (their one-third share). Physical description: middle height, fair, long face, scar on the middle of his forehead.

sarapas-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.bio: Brother of Achilleus. Co-freed the slave Apollonous (their one-third share) in 91 CE. Physical description: middle height, fair, long face, scar on left [damaged].

heraclas-son-of-tryphon-oxyrhynchus-ca-6.bio: Paid 200 drachmae of imperial silver coin plus copper talents as ransom for the slave Apollonous in 91 CE. Aged about 31. Physical description: middle height, fair, long face, scar above right knee. His relationship to Apollonous is not specified in the document.

serenus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.patronymic: freedman of Apollonianus son of Sarapion
serenus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Freedman who became a substantial creditor, lending 900 silver drachmae secured by a slave. Petitioned the archidicastes Antoninus (also called Pudens) in Alexandria to enforce repayment after the borrower defaulted, demonstrating the legal standing and economic agency available to freedpersons in Roman Egypt.
serenus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.disambiguation: Not otherwise attested in the Oxyrhynchus papyri.

dioscorus-freedman-oxyrhynchus-2nd-centu.patronymic: freedman of Sarapion son of Sarapion
dioscorus-freedman-oxyrhynchus-2nd-centu.bio: Freedman who applied for a license to practice the trade of river-worker in Oxyrhynchus in 128-129 CE, during Hadrian's reign. Demonstrates economic integration of freedpersons after manumission.
dioscorus-freedman-oxyrhynchus-2nd-centu.disambiguation: Not the Dioscorus son of Komon who was Aloine's brother.

ophelas-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Freedman of a female owner (Apia/Dionysia), who leased three arourae of state land for five years in 139 CE. Also known by the nickname Corax ("Crow"). His case parallels Euphrosyne's in that both were freed by female owners.

dionysous-freedwoman-oxyrhynchus-2nd-cen.patronymic: freedwoman of Dionysia daughter of Dionysius
dionysous-freedwoman-oxyrhynchus-2nd-cen.bio: Freedwoman who declared her son Ptollis eligible for the 12-drachma tax class in 133 CE, demonstrating that freedwomen's children could attain privileged metropolitan status in Oxyrhynchus.

aphroditous-oxyrhynchus-early-2nd-centur.patronymic: freedwoman of Epicrates son of Epicrates
aphroditous-oxyrhynchus-early-2nd-centur.bio: Freedwoman adopted by her former master Epicrates, becoming his legal daughter. Sold 6 2/3 arourae of catoecic land for 1,000 silver drachmae. A remarkable case of social integration after manumission, with the former master-slave relationship transformed into a legal parent-child relationship.

syntyche-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.patronymic: freedwoman of Sarapias daughter of Dionysius
syntyche-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Freedwoman who received grain transfers at the village of Seryphis in 135 CE, demonstrating economic participation by freedwomen in the commodity economy.

abascantus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.patronymic: freedman of Samus son of Heraclides
abascantus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Freedman who served as legal guardian for the children of Theon in 186 CE, a position of trust that indicates high social standing despite freed status.

plousia-oxyrhynchus-late-1st-century-ce.bio: Slave woman, about 23 years old, sold by Serapous daughter of Zoilus to Harthoonis (also called Theon) for 10 talents 5,000 drachmae in bronze. Her dual name may indicate prior ownership changes. Sold through the same Chaeremon-agoranomus channel as Euphrosyne's manumission.

julia-heracla-oxyrhynchus-1st-century-ce.bio: Roman citizen woman in Oxyrhynchus who ceded property to her daughter in 95 CE. Her Roman citizenship conferred legal capacities different from those of Greek-status women like Aloine.

victor-son-of-cornelius-unknown-egypt-6t.bio: Monk of the monastery of Apa Makrobios who freed his house-born slave Menas on 14 July 589 CE, "in order to find remission and redemption of all my sins." A late antique Christian manumission showing the continuity of house-born slavery into the Byzantine period.

psates-son-of-abraamios-panopolis-origin.dates: fl. 592 CE, d. before July 600 CE
psates-son-of-abraamios-panopolis-origin.bio: Purple-seller who migrated from Panopolis (Akhmim) to the village of This in the Thinite nome of Upper Egypt. Resident for a long time by 592 CE. Sold a dilapidated house to his son Pachymios in that year. Died between 592 and 600 CE. His mother was Tmouthes. Illiterate; a deacon wrote on his behalf.
psates-son-of-abraamios-panopolis-origin.disambiguation: Father of Pachymios, Ioannes, and Tarsene. Not to be confused with the deacon Psates son of Papnouthios who served as scribe in the 592 sale.

maria-daughter-of-philamon-panopolis-ori.dates: fl. 592 CE, d. before July 600 CE
maria-daughter-of-philamon-panopolis-ori.bio: Wife of Psates son of Abraamios. Daughter of Philamon, whose mother was Taniskene. Originally from Panopolis, resident in This. Co-seller of the family house in 592 CE. Died between 592 and 600 CE. Illiterate.
maria-daughter-of-philamon-panopolis-ori.disambiguation: Mother of Pachymios, Ioannes, and Tarsene. Not to be confused with the Maria who is named as mother of Pyras daughter of Besatas in P.Paris 21 (616 CE), a different individual.

pachymios-son-of-psates-panopolis-origin.bio: Eldest son of Psates and Maria. Purple-seller who inherited and expanded the family business in This. Bought a dilapidated house from his parents in 592 CE. After their deaths, confirmed as legitimate owner in the 600 CE inheritance division. Ran a workshop with multiple hired purple-workers by 605 CE. Lent money to village workers in 606 and 613 CE. Purchased an adjacent house in 616 CE, consolidating his property holdings. Active across the reigns of Maurice, Phocas, and Heraclius.
pachymios-son-of-psates-panopolis-origin.disambiguation: Son of Psates son of Abraamios. Name appears in various spellings (Pachymios, Pachymis, Pachoumis) across the family dossier.

ioannes-son-of-psates-panopolis-this-thi.bio: Second son of Psates and Maria, full brother of Pachymios and Tarsene. Purple-seller. Participated in the inheritance division of 600 CE, receiving one-third of the estate and acknowledging Pachymios's prior house purchase.
ioannes-son-of-psates-panopolis-this-thi.disambiguation: Possibly identical with Ioannes son of Abraamios, priest from This, who witnessed the 606 CE wine loan (SB 1.4505, corpus ID 6313), if the patronymic refers to the grandfather. Unconfirmed.

tarsene-daughter-of-psates-panopolis-thi.bio: Daughter of Psates and Maria, full sister of Pachymios and Ioannes. Described as a widow in the 600 CE inheritance division. Received one-third of the parental estate, equal to her brothers' shares.

dioskoros-son-of-harsynios-this-thinite-.bio: Hired purple-worker who contracted with Pachymios in January 605 CE for a two-year term. Paid in grain (19 artabai over 2 years) plus food. His mother Tibellas co-signed the contract.
dioskoros-son-of-harsynios-this-thinite-.disambiguation: Not to be confused with the notary Dioskoros who drafted the 600 CE inheritance division (P.Paris 20).

tibellas-this-thinite-nome-fl-605-ce.bio: Mother of the hired purple-worker Dioskoros son of Harsynios. Co-signatory of his employment contract with Pachymios in 605 CE, possibly serving as guarantor.

kallinikos-son-of-senouthes-this-thinite.bio: Hired worker in the village of This who borrowed gold from Pachymios on two occasions (606 and 613 CE), repaying in wine from local vineyards. His mother was Tloullas.

senouthes-the-builder-this-thinite-nome-.bio: Builder from the village of This who assessed the value of the dilapidated house sold by Psates and Maria to Pachymios in 592 CE, setting the price at three nomismata less one keration.
senouthes-the-builder-this-thinite-nome-.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Senouthes son of Phaliout, a prior property owner, nor with the father of Kallinikos, also named Senouthes.

psates-son-of-papnouthios-this-thinite-n.bio: Deacon from This who served as scribe for the 592 CE house sale, writing on behalf of the illiterate Psates son of Abraamios and Maria.
psates-son-of-papnouthios-this-thinite-n.disambiguation: Not the same person as Psates son of Abraamios the purple-seller. Shares the common name Psates but has a different patronymic.

besatas-son-of-elias-this-thinite-nome-f.bio: Deacon from This who witnessed the 592 CE house sale. Possibly the father of Elias son of Besatas, priest, who witnessed the 616 CE sale.
besatas-son-of-elias-this-thinite-nome-f.disambiguation: Possibly father of Elias son of Besatas, priest (P.Paris 21, corpus ID 6310, 616 CE).

athanasios-son-of-paamios-this-thinite-n.bio: Priest from This who witnessed the 592 CE house sale.

ioulios-the-notary-this-thinite-nome-fl-.bio: Professional notary who drafted the 592 CE house sale document in This.

viktor-son-of-psaios-this-thinite-nome-b.bio: Prior owner of the property in This that Psates purchased and later sold to Pachymios. Named alongside Senouthes son of Phaliout as source of the two old sale documents.

senouthes-son-of-phaliout-this-thinite-n.bio: Prior owner of the property in This, co-seller with Viktor son of Psaios.
senouthes-son-of-phaliout-this-thinite-n.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Senouthes the builder who assessed the property value in 592 CE.

pebos-son-of-horos-this-thinite-nome-fl-.bio: Property owner in This whose house appears as a boundary marker in both the 592 and 616 CE sales, indicating long-term residence in the village.

pyras-daughter-of-besatas-this-thinite-n.bio: Daughter of Besatas, whose mother was Maria. Sold a house in This to Pachymios in 616 CE, acting also on behalf of her sister Rose.

rose-daughter-of-besatas-this-thinite-no.bio: Sister of Pyras daughter of Besatas. Co-seller of a house in This to Pachymios in 616 CE, represented by her sister Pyras.

antheria-daughter-of-senouthes-this-thin.bio: Daughter of Senouthes, whose mother was Kyra. Co-seller of a house in This to Pachymios in 616 CE alongside Pyras and Rose.

elias-son-of-besatas-this-thinite-nome-f.bio: Priest from This who witnessed the 616 CE house sale. Possibly the son of the deacon Besatas son of Elias who witnessed the 592 CE sale.
elias-son-of-besatas-this-thinite-nome-f.disambiguation: If son of the 592 deacon, the name-reversal pattern (father Besatas son of Elias, son Elias son of Besatas) and clerical promotion is consistent with a generational transition.

mousaios-son-of-papnouthios-this-thinite.bio: Priest from This who witnessed the 616 CE house sale. Possibly related to the deacon Psates son of Papnouthios who served as scribe in 592 CE.
mousaios-son-of-papnouthios-this-thinite.disambiguation: Shares the patronymic Papnouthios with the 592 scribe, but common names make the connection uncertain.

ioannes-son-of-abraamios-priest-this-thi.bio: Priest from This who witnessed the 606 CE wine loan. Possibly identical with Ioannes son of Psates son of Abraamios, the brother of Pachymios, if the patronymic refers to the grandfather.
ioannes-son-of-abraamios-priest-this-thi.disambiguation: The identification with Pachymios's brother remains uncertain.

chariston-the-copper-worker-panopolis-or.bio: Copper-worker listed as shared property in the 600 CE inheritance division. Legal status (enslaved, debt-bonded, or contracted) unclear from document.
chariston-the-copper-worker-panopolis-or.disambiguation: Distinguished from another Chariston the ironworker in the same document.

chariston-the-ironworker-panopolis-or-th.bio: Ironworker listed as shared property in the 600 CE inheritance division. Legal status unclear.
chariston-the-ironworker-panopolis-or-th.disambiguation: Distinguished from Chariston the copper-worker in the same document.

roles.title: mother of Psates son of Abraamios

tmouthes-panopolis-uncertain.bio: Mother of Psates son of Abraamios, named in the 592 CE sale document per the standard matronymic identification formula.

roles.title: mother of Maria daughter of Philamon

taniskene-panopolis-uncertain.bio: Mother of Maria daughter of Philamon, named in the 592 CE sale document.

tloullas-this-thinite-nome-fl-606-613-ce.bio: Mother of Kallinikos son of Senouthes, named in the matronymic identification formula of both wine loan documents (606 and 613 CE).

monathios-son-of-pamidos-this-thinite-no.bio: Property owner in This whose house appears as the western boundary marker in the 616 CE sale to Pachymios.

mousaios-son-of-psaros-this-thinite-nome.bio: Priest from This whose house appears as the southern boundary marker in the 616 CE sale to Pachymios.
mousaios-son-of-psaros-this-thinite-nome.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Mousaios son of Papnouthios, the priest who witnessed the same 616 sale.

apellis-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man who inscribed a lead curse tablet (defixio) out of love for Eunikos in the Greek colony of Gela, Sicily. The tablet names rival lovers, curses chorus leaders (khoragoi), and is one of the earliest documented declarations of same-sex love in the Greek world. A man of the same name appears in a near-contemporary legal text from Gela (ISic030016), where Anthemokritos bade Apellis offer security in a dispute over cattle prices; if this is the same individual, he was involved in the commercial and legal life of the community as well as its erotic and magical spheres.
apellis-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.disambiguation: Possibly identical with the Apellis named in ISic030016 (Corpus ID 8389), a legal text from Gela dated c. 500-475 BCE. Not the Apelles son of Zopyros named as priest of Apollo in Tauromenion decrees (Corpus IDs 12619, 12620), who dates to the 1st century BCE.

eunikos-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man for whose love Apellis composed a lead curse tablet in Greek Sicily. His name means 'good victory' (eu + nike). The tablet prays for Eunikos's victory 'always, everywhere' and curses all his rivals and the competing chorus leaders. He appears to have been connected to choral performance -- either as a performer or as the object of desire within the competitive world of choral festivals. Multiple men competed for his attention, including Kaledias and possibly others.
eunikos-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.disambiguation: Not otherwise attested in the Sicilian epigraphic record.

kaledias-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man named on Apellis's curse tablet as a rival to be kept apart from Apellis. The curse specifically targets Kaledias to prevent him from interfering in the relationship between Apellis and Eunikos. His precise relationship to the choral competitions is unclear.

sosias-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man cursed on Apellis's tablet 'because of his love of Melanthios.' Identified as 'the one from the shop (kapeleion) of the Alkiadai,' a commercial landmark in Gela. His love for Melanthios, described with the same word (philotati) that Apellis uses for his love of Eunikos, reveals a second pair of male lovers operating within the same social network.

melanthios-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man loved by Sosias, named on Apellis's curse tablet. His name means 'of the dark flowers.' A man of the same name appears on ISic030032 (Corpus ID 8420), another curse tablet from the same region dated c. 460-440 BCE, listed among individuals 'inscribed for failure.' If this is the same person, he was a repeated target of magical cursing.
melanthios-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.disambiguation: Possibly identical with the Melanthios named on ISic030032 (Corpus ID 8420), a curse tablet dated c. 460-440 BCE.

pyrias-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man cursed on Apellis's tablet alongside Mysskelos and Damophantos, together with their unnamed khoragos (chorus leader). Appears to be a member of a rival choral troupe competing against Eunikos's group.

mysskelos-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man cursed on Apellis's tablet alongside Pyrias and Damophantos, together with their unnamed khoragos. Member of a rival choral troupe. His name may be related to 'myschelos,' a term whose exact derivation is uncertain.
mysskelos-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.disambiguation: Not otherwise attested with certainty. A Myschelos appears in ISic020581 (Corpus ID 8400), 'The lands of Myschelos,' possibly the same individual.

damophantos-gela-sicily-c-475-450-bce.bio: Man cursed on Apellis's tablet alongside Pyrias and Mysskelos, together with their unnamed khoragos. Member of a rival choral troupe. His name means 'appearing before the people' (damos + phanein), suggesting civic prominence.

porkos-gela-sicily-c-499-480-bce.bio: Man who inscribed a drinking cup (skyphos) with a declaration of love for a woman named Phryna, returning the cup to the thiasos (drinking fellowship). The inscription concludes with a crude sexual joke about sodomy. The text provides evidence for sympotic culture and erotic discourse in late archaic Gela.

geloios-gela-sicily-c-499-480-bce.bio: Man who declared his love for Aka on a sympotic graffito, repeating the declaration twice. The inscription adds 'but it is Eumachos who pays,' suggesting a complex erotic-economic arrangement. A man of the same name appears on ISic030032 (Corpus ID 8420), a curse tablet listing names 'for failure,' which may indicate overlapping social networks.
geloios-gela-sicily-c-499-480-bce.disambiguation: Possibly identical with the Geloios named on ISic030032 (Corpus ID 8420).

eumachos-gela-sicily-c-499-480-bce.bio: Man named in the Geloios-Aka graffito as 'the one who pays' (misthotai). His role is ambiguous: he may be a symposium host, a patron financing the social gathering, or a man paying for sexual or romantic access within the sympotic economy.

gorgidas-thebes-boeotia-fl-378-bce.bio: Theban commander who organized the Sacred Band (hieros lochos) as three hundred chosen men, maintained at public expense and quartered in the Cadmeia. Under his original deployment the Band was distributed among the front ranks of the entire phalanx. Present at the liberation of Thebes in 379/378 BCE, where he and Epaminondas presented the liberators to the popular assembly.
gorgidas-thebes-boeotia-fl-378-bce.disambiguation: Not otherwise well attested outside Plutarch's Life of Pelopidas.

pelopidas-thebes-boeotia-c-410-364-bce.bio: Wealthy Theban aristocrat who led the conspiracy to liberate Thebes from Spartan occupation in 379/378 BCE. As captain of the Sacred Band, unified the unit and deployed it as a single body rather than distributing it across the phalanx. Won the first Theban victory over Sparta at Tegyra (375 BCE) and led the Sacred Band's decisive charge at Leuctra (371 BCE). Died at the Battle of Cynoscephalae in Thessaly (364 BCE) fighting Alexander of Pherae.

epaminondas-thebes-boeotia-c-418-362-bce.bio: Theban general and philosopher-statesman, friend and co-commander of Pelopidas. Inventor of the oblique phalanx, which he used to devastating effect at Leuctra (371 BCE). Led four invasions of the Peloponnese, liberated Messenia from Spartan control, and founded the cities of Megalopolis and Messene. Died at the Second Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE). Plutarch describes him as Pelopidas' opposite: ascetic, philosophical, devoted to the life of the mind.

philip-ii-of-macedon-pella-macedonia-the.location: Pella, Macedonia; Thebes (as hostage, c. 368-365 BCE)
philip-ii-of-macedon-pella-macedonia-the.bio: King of Macedon who as a boy lived in Thebes as a hostage in the household of Pammenes, where he observed Epaminondas' military innovations and the Sacred Band. Later built the Macedonian army that annihilated the Sacred Band at Chaeroneia (338 BCE). Wept over the dead of the Sacred Band and cursed anyone who would call their love disgraceful. Assassinated in 336 BCE.

roles.title: commander of left wing at Chaeroneia

alexander-iii-the-great-pella-macedonia-.bio: Son of Philip II. At eighteen years of age commanded the Macedonian left wing at Chaeroneia, leading the Companion cavalry against the Theban position and the Sacred Band. Later destroyed the city of Thebes entirely in 335 BCE as punishment for rebellion.

roles.title: host of young Philip of Macedon

pammenes-thebes-boeotia-fl-370s-340s-bce.bio: Theban general in whose household the young Philip of Macedon lived as a hostage. Cited by Plutarch for the quip that Homer's Nestor should have stationed lover by beloved rather than clansman by clansman. His tactical thinking reflected the Theban conviction that erotic bonds produced superior military cohesion.

gnathios-athens-5th-c-bce.bio: Man named in IG I3 1399, an inscription recording a formal oath sworn by a man who had fallen in love with a boy, pledging to share in strife and tearful war. The monument is described as sacred to a hero whose name is lost. Gnathios' soul is said to have "perished" — possibly indicating he died in the war he pledged to share.

cleombrotus-i-sparta-d-371-bce.bio: Spartan king who led the invasion of Boeotia in 371 BCE and was killed at the Battle of Leuctra when the Sacred Band struck his position before he could complete his tactical redeployment.

harmodius-athens-d-514-bce.bio: Athenian youth, the beloved (eromenos) of Aristogeiton, who together assassinated the tyrant Hipparchus in 514 BCE. Celebrated in Athenian civic religion as a model of virtuous male love directed toward the service of the state. Invoked by Aeschines as an exemplar of "chaste and lawful" love.

aristogeiton-athens-d-514-bce.bio: Athenian citizen, the lover (erastes) of Harmodius, who together assassinated the tyrant Hipparchus in 514 BCE. Thucydides says the act was motivated by a love affair — Hipparchus had made advances on Harmodius and insulted his family when rejected.

theon-son-of-proechia-egypt-5th-century-.bio: Man who commissioned an erotic charm to bind Euphemia daughter of Dorothea in mad love (*eros maniodes*) for ten months beginning on Hathyr 25, second indiction. The spell was deposited in a grave, commanding the restless dead to seek out Euphemia and bring her to Theon's feet. Identified by matronymic, standard in magical texts.

euphemia-daughter-of-dorothea-egypt-5th-.bio: Woman targeted by Theon's erotic binding spell. The spell sought to bind her brain, hands, inner organs, genitals, and heart, to prevent her from eating, drinking, sleeping, or laughing, and to force her to abandon her family and any other lover. Identified only by matronymic.

horion-son-of-sarapous-egypt-2nd-century.bio: Commissioner of an erotic charm compelling Nike daughter of Apollonoutos to fall in love with Pantoutos son of Tmesios for five months. The spell was deposited beside a mummy.

nike-daughter-of-apollonoutos-egypt-2nd-.bio: Woman targeted by a love spell commissioned by Horion. The spell sought to compel her to fall in love with Pantoutos for five months.

pantoutos-son-of-tmesios-egypt-2nd-centu.bio: Man for whose benefit the love spell against Nike was commissioned. Whether he was involved in commissioning the spell or was the object of a third party's arrangement is unclear from the text.

serenus-oxyrhynchus-egypt-2nd-century-ce.bio: Man who wrote a letter of desperate anguish to his estranged wife or sister-wife Isidora. After her departure, he stopped bathing for a full month (from Phaophi 12 to Hathyr 12). His letter mentions accusations of prostitution involving a man named Colobus, and counter-accusations that Serenus sold Isidora's chain and put her on a boat.

isidora-oxyrhynchus-egypt-2nd-century-ce.bio: Woman addressed as "beloved sister" by Serenus. She left him and sent letters that "would have shaken a stone." She accused a man named Colobus of having made her a prostitute. Her side of the correspondence does not survive.

sarapammon-son-of-area-antinoopolis-egyp.bio: Commissioner of the famous Antinoopolis love spell, which targeted Ptolemais daughter of Aias (Horigenes). The archaeological find included a clay figurine pierced with thirteen needles and a lead tablet inscribed with the binding formula, deposited together in a grave. The spell follows the formulary of PGM IV.296-466.

ptolemais-daughter-of-aias-antinoopolis-.bio: Woman targeted by the Antinoopolis binding spell commissioned by Sarapammon. A clay figurine representing her, kneeling with bound hands and pierced by thirteen needles, was found in a grave beside the inscribed lead tablet.

isidora-oxyrhynchus-fl-2nd-c-ce.bio: Wife (and possibly sister) of Serenus. Left her husband, wrote him letters fierce enough to "shake a stone," accused him through the intermediary Colobus of selling her gold chain and making her a prostitute. Did not return.
isidora-oxyrhynchus-fl-2nd-c-ce.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Isidora daughter of Herakleides (BGU 4.1050) or other Isidoras in the Oxyrhynchus papyri.

herakleides-elephantine-fl-310-bce.bio: Temnitan man who married Demetria the Koan in 310 BCE. Bound by contract not to bring another wife or have children by another woman.
herakleides-elephantine-fl-310-bce.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Herakleides the archidikastes (P.Oxy. 2.281).

isidora-daughter-of-herakleides-alexandr.patronymic: daughter of Herakleides also called Dionysios
isidora-daughter-of-herakleides-alexandr.bio: Woman who married Dionysios under a contract that prohibited mistreatment, abuse, expulsion, or bringing another woman into the house. Brought gold earrings and 60 drachmae as dowry.
isidora-daughter-of-herakleides-alexandr.disambiguation: Not the same as Isidora wife of Serenus (P.Oxy. 3.528).

hermotimus-of-pedasa-pedasa-caria-persia.bio: A native of Pedasa in Caria, near Halicarnassus, who was captured by enemies, sold to the slave-trader Panionius of Chios, castrated, and resold to the Persian court. Rose to become the most favoured eunuch of Xerxes I. Entrusted with the guardianship of the king's sons during the invasion of Greece in 480 BCE. Achieved what Herodotus called "the fullest vengeance for wrong done to him than had any man whom we know" by compelling Panionius to castrate his own four sons, and then compelling the sons to castrate their father.

roles.title: slave-trader specialising in castration of boys

panionius-of-chios-chios-sardis-ephesus-.bio: A man from the island of Chios who earned his living by procuring beautiful boys, castrating them, and selling them at high prices in Sardis and Ephesus, where the Persian market for eunuchs created strong demand. Among his victims was Hermotimus of Pedasa. Later found by Hermotimus at Atarneus in Mysia with his wife and four sons, and subjected to a revenge in which he was compelled to castrate his own children, who then castrated him in turn. Herodotus describes his trade as "most wicked practices."

bagoas-the-younger-persian-court-alexand.bio: A Persian eunuch who served first in the court of Darius III and, after Darius's defeat and murder in 330 BCE, passed into Alexander the Great's household. Described as "in the flower of his youth." Won a prize for song and dance at a festival; Alexander kissed him publicly at the crowd's demand (Plutarch). Exercised significant political influence at Alexander's court according to Curtius Rufus. The satrap Orxines, who insulted Bagoas by refusing to pay respect to "men used as women," was manoeuvred into execution. Historical existence debated by some scholars.
bagoas-the-younger-persian-court-alexand.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Bagoas the elder, a eunuch who was a powerful vizier under Artaxerxes III and involved in regicide.

favorinus-of-arles-arelate-arles-gaul-ro.bio: Born in Arelate (Arles), Gaul, around 80 CE. Described by his rival Polemo as "a eunuch born without testicles" and by Philostratus as a hermaphrodite; modern scholars suggest a possible intersex condition. Despite his beardless face and high voice, became one of the most celebrated orators of the Second Sophistic. Friend of the emperor Hadrian, from whom he later fell out of favour. Famous for three self-proclaimed paradoxes: that as a Gaul he spoke Greek; that as a eunuch he was tried for adultery; and that having quarrelled with an emperor he was still alive. His career demonstrates that ancient gender categories, while fiercely policed, could be overcome by individual talent and ambition.
favorinus-of-arles-arelate-arles-gaul-ro.disambiguation: The Favorinus who appears in Plutarch's dialogues (e.g., Concerning the First Principles of Cold) and is cited frequently in Diogenes Laertius's Lives of Eminent Philosophers is the same individual.

polemo-of-laodicea-laodicea-on-the-lycus.bio: A celebrated sophist from Laodicea who settled in Smyrna and became one of the leading orators of the Second Sophistic. Author of a treatise on physiognomy that catalogued the bodily signs of gender deviance, including the gait, voice, and deportment of the kinaidos. Rival of Favorinus of Arles, whom he attacked in both person and writing. His physiognomic analysis is a key source for understanding how Roman masculinity was constructed as a performed achievement rather than a biological given.
polemo-of-laodicea-laodicea-on-the-lycus.disambiguation: Not to be confused with Polemon I of Pontus, the Hellenistic king.

attis-phrygia-pessinus-mythological.bio: Mythological Phrygian youth beloved by Cybele (the Great Mother). In the dominant version of the myth (preserved by Pausanias), born from an almond tree that grew from the severed male organ of the hermaphroditic demon Agdistis. Attis's beauty surpassed the human. When Agdistis disrupted his wedding, Attis went mad and castrated himself, dying beneath a pine tree. Agdistis persuaded Zeus to preserve his body from decay. The annual Roman festival of Attis, culminating on March 24 (Dies Sanguinis), re-enacted his castration. An alternative tradition (Hermesianax) described Attis as "a eunuch from birth."
attis-phrygia-pessinus-mythological.disambiguation: Not to be confused with the historical Attis mentioned by Plutarch in the Life of Sertorius (corpus ID 98257), an Arcadian killed by a boar.

combabos-hierapolis-syria-mythologicalle.bio: A legendary figure described by Lucian in De Dea Syria as the origin of the Galli priesthood at Hierapolis. Sent by the Assyrian king to oversee the construction of a temple with the queen Stratonice, Combabos preemptively castrated himself and sealed his genitals in a box, which he left with the king. When Stratonice fell in love with him and he was accused of adultery, the box was opened to prove his innocence. According to Lucian, his closest friends then castrated themselves out of sympathy, establishing the tradition of the Galli.

masabates-persian-court-fl-c-401-bce.bio: A eunuch of the Persian king Artaxerxes II who cut off the head and right hand of the dead prince Cyrus the Younger after the Battle of Cunaxa in 401 BCE. The queen mother Parysatis, who favoured Cyrus, plotted revenge. She won Masabates in a dice game with the king, then had him flayed alive, his body set up on three stakes and his skin nailed to a fourth. Artaxerxes was furious but unable to undo the act. The episode illustrates both the vulnerability and the expendability of eunuchs in the Persian court.

roles.title: eunuch attendant to Cyrus the Younger

pariscas-battlefield-of-cunaxa-fl-c-401-.bio: A eunuch who attended Cyrus the Younger and mourned over his dead body on the battlefield of Cunaxa. When Artasyras, the king's Eye, asked who lay dead, Pariscas replied: "Dost thou not see Cyrus dead?" Described by Plutarch as one of Cyrus's most trusted attendants. His vigil over the body exemplifies the fidelity that Xenophon theorised in the Cyropaedia.

satibarzanes-battlefield-of-cunaxa-fl-c-.bio: A eunuch who served Artaxerxes II and who, after the king was wounded at Cunaxa, "ran about in quest of a drink for him." He found only vile water in a wretched skin carried by a Caunian, about two quarts in all. The king drank it gratefully. A minor figure whose act of devotion illustrates the intimate service eunuchs provided to Persian kings.

arnaces-salamis-fl-c-480-bce.bio: A Persian royal eunuch captured by the Greeks during the invasion of 480 BCE. Themistocles used him as a messenger to deceive Xerxes, sending him to tell the king that the Greeks planned to destroy the bridge at the Hellespont, thus encouraging Xerxes to retreat. The episode illustrates how eunuchs served as intermediaries between powers -- trusted by virtue of their status, useful precisely because their loyalties were assumed to follow power rather than principle.

roles.title: treasurer to Candace, queen of Ethiopia

ethiopian-eunuch-road-from-jerusalem-to-.bio: An unnamed eunuch described in Acts 8 as "a eunuch of great authority under Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was over all her treasure." A court eunuch in the traditional pattern -- powerful, wealthy, managing a queen's finances. Had travelled to Jerusalem to worship, suggesting Jewish sympathies, but would have been excluded from the Temple under Deuteronomy 23:1. Philip the Evangelist baptised him on the road to Gaza after finding him reading Isaiah 53 in his chariot. The passage he was reading -- about a man whose "generation" is cut off -- resonates with the eunuch's own condition. His baptism represents the early Christian inclusion of those excluded by traditional Jewish law.

apellis-gela-sicily-fl-500--450-bce.bio: A resident of Gela who appears in two lead documents: a commercial letter (ISic030016) where he is asked to stand surety in a cattle transaction, and a curse tablet (ISic030019) where he curses rival chorus-leaders to ensure victory for his beloved Eunikos. If the same individual, Apellis was both a trusted commercial figure and a practitioner of competitive magic. He is unusual among curse commissioners for naming himself.
apellis-gela-sicily-fl-500--450-bce.disambiguation: The commercial letter (500--475 BCE) and the curse tablet (475--450 BCE) may refer to the same person or to different men bearing the same name across one generation. The identification remains speculative.

eunikos-gela-sicily-fl-475--450-bce.bio: The beloved of Apellis and the beneficiary of the Gela curse tablet (ISic030019). Apellis cursed all rival chorus-leaders (khoragoi) and their families so that Eunikos would be praised and admired by all, "willingly and unwillingly," and would achieve "victory always, everywhere." His role in the choregic competitions -- whether as performer, competing choregos, or favoured protege -- is debated.
eunikos-gela-sicily-fl-475--450-bce.disambiguation: Known only from the Apellis curse tablet. No other attestation.

roles.title: Target of curse combining illness and failure

chamis-selinus-selinunte-sicily-fl-499--.bio: The target of a curse tablet from Selinus (ISic030010) that reads: "Chamis, I hold you, and with illness's turning-point, for failure." The curse combines medical harm with practical aims, holding Chamis in the crisis of disease so that he cannot achieve his objectives. Nothing else is known of him.
chamis-selinus-selinunte-sicily-fl-499--.disambiguation: Known only from this single defixio.

kerdon-kamarina-sicily-fl-460--440-bce.bio: Listed first among over a dozen targets on the Kamarina curse tablet (ISic030032). His name, meaning "the profitable one" (from kerdos, "gain"), suggests he may have been an artisan or trader. Cursed to be "bloodless and wretched" along with all others named on the tablet.
kerdon-kamarina-sicily-fl-460--440-bce.disambiguation: Known only from the Kamarina curse tablet. A common name in the Greek world.

puthon-son-of-diokles-kamarina-sicily-fl.bio: Named on the Kamarina curse tablet (ISic030032) with his patronymic, "son of Diokles," providing precise identification within the community. Cursed to be bloodless and wretched.
puthon-son-of-diokles-kamarina-sicily-fl.disambiguation: Known only from the Kamarina curse tablet.

sosias-gela-andor-kamarina-sicily-fl-480.patronymic: unknown (possibly son of Archonidas or son of Archias)

roles.title: Target of Apellis curse (associated with shop of the Alkiadai)

sosias-gela-andor-kamarina-sicily-fl-480.bio: Named on the Apellis curse tablet (ISic030019) as "the one from the shop of the Alkiadai," cursed "because of his love of Melanthios." A Sosias also appears on the Kamarina name-list (ISic030031) twice: "Sosias son of Archonidas" and "Sosias son of Archias." Whether the same individual is referenced across these tablets is uncertain.
sosias-gela-andor-kamarina-sicily-fl-480.disambiguation: Sosias is a common Greek name. The Gela and Kamarina references may involve different individuals.

kaledias-gela-sicily-fl-475--450-bce.bio: Named on the Apellis curse tablet (ISic030019) as a person Apellis wishes to keep apart from himself -- apparently a rival for the affections of Eunikos. Apellis writes: "I mark down Kaledias to keep him apart from Apellis."
kaledias-gela-sicily-fl-475--450-bce.disambiguation: Known only from the Apellis curse tablet.

myskon-son-of-damis-gela-sicily-fl-500--.patronymic: son of Damis son of Kobetos
myskon-son-of-damis-gela-sicily-fl-500--.bio: Named in a commercial lead letter from Gela (ISic030016) as a party in a cattle transaction that apparently failed. He was found "sitting along the avenue" and "came not having the money, the price of the cattle; he did not buy." His grandfather was Kobetos. The name Myskon may be related to Mysskelos on the Apellis curse tablet.
myskon-son-of-damis-gela-sicily-fl-500--.disambiguation: The possible connection between Myskon (ISic030016) and Mysskelos (ISic030019) is speculative.

dameas-son-of-aristodamos-messana-messin.bio: Named first on a lead curse tablet from Messana (ISic030330), a simple name-list deposited in a tomb. The tablet lists five or six individuals by name and patronymic, with no accompanying curse formula.

arian-oxyrhynchus-egypt-probable-fl-4th-.bio: Woman who suffered from recurring fevers and for whom a Gnostic-Christian healing amulet was produced. Described as "a servant of the living God." The amulet specified protection from daily, nightly, and slight fevers. Nothing else is known of her life.
arian-oxyrhynchus-egypt-probable-fl-4th-.disambiguation: Known only from P.Oxy. inv. 2065 (DCLP 65.64394). Not to be confused with the Arian heresy or any historical figure named Arius.

joannia-daughter-of-anastasia-oxyrhynchu.bio: Woman who suffered from fevers (quotidian, tertian, quartan) and for whom a Christian healing amulet was produced, invoking Christ, the Virgin Mary, the archangels, and local Egyptian saints. Her mother had two names: Anastasia, also called Euphemia. The amulet was likely produced by a member of the clergy.

vibius-son-of-gennaia-egypt-fl-6th-centu.bio: Man with a Latin name who suffered from daily and quotidian fevers. A Christian healing amulet was produced for him, embedding the healing command within a Christological creed. His Greek-named mother Gennaia suggests a mixed Roman-Greek background.

ahmed-son-of-mariam-egypt-fl-post-641-ce.bio: Muslim man who suffered from fever, burning, mania, and weeping, for whom a Coptic-speaking practitioner produced a healing amulet invoking archangels and voces magicae. His Arabic name and Coptic amulet demonstrate cross-communal magical practice in post-conquest Egypt.
ahmed-son-of-mariam-egypt-fl-post-641-ce.disambiguation: Known only from P.Heid. inv. Kopt. 544b (DCLP 99.98048).

antonia-sokratike-attica-greece-roman-im.bio: Woman who commissioned a tomb monument (heroon) for her deceased husband Antiochos (also called Synesios), protecting it with a curse invoking chthonic gods and threatening violators with shivering, fever, quartan ague, and elephantiasis.

antiochos-synesios-attica-greece-roman-i.bio: Deceased husband of Antonia Sokratike, described as her "sweetest husband" (glykytatos aner). A heroon was built for him and protected by a curse invoking chthonic gods.

neoteros-son-of-marreous-karanis-fayum-e.bio: Resident of the Fayum (probably Karanis) who submitted a written oracle question to the god Soxis Pnepheros in the eighth year of Tiberius (21-22 CE), asking whether he should travel to Alexandria to bring a legal complaint. His name, Neoteros ("Younger"), suggests he was distinguished from an older relative of the same name. His patronymic Marreous is Egyptian.
neoteros-son-of-marreous-karanis-fayum-e.disambiguation: Known only from this single oracle question.

asklepiades-son-of-areios-soknopaiou-nes.bio: Man who submitted a written oracle question to the god Soknopaios on 26 April 6 CE, asking whether he was permitted to marry Tapetheus daughter of Marres, who had previously been the wife of Horion. His Greek name and patronymic suggest Hellenised background; his consultation of an Egyptian crocodile god shows cultural integration.
asklepiades-son-of-areios-soknopaiou-nes.disambiguation: Known only from this oracle question.

tapetheus-daughter-of-marres-soknopaiou-.bio: Woman whose proposed marriage to Asklepiades son of Areios was the subject of an oracle question to Soknopaios in 6 CE. Previously married to Horion. Her Egyptian name (Tapetheus) contrasts with her suitor's Greek name.

roles.title: subject of Christian oracle question (proposed marriage)

theodora-krokodilopolis-arsinoe-fayum-eg.bio: Woman described as "your servant" (of God) whose proposed marriage to Joseph was the subject of a Christian oracle question. The answer was "Yes. Yes." Nothing else is known about her.

didymus-son-of-unknown-egypt-possibly-ox.bio: Son who wrote to his father Chaeremon about estate management and family news. His mother Thaesis had gone to Antinoopolis for a funeral. His letter includes the standard evil eye protection formula for the children of a man named Phullon.

ammonios-xystophorus-antinoopolis-egypt-.bio: Man writing from Antinoopolis to his brother Andronikos about letters of introduction and household management. His letter includes the evil eye protection formula for Andronikos's children. The epithet "xystophorus" in the address may indicate an athletic or military role.

roles.title: priest and stolistes, temple of Soknopaiou Nesos

aurelius-pakysis-son-of-tesenouphis-sokn.bio: Priest and stolistes (vestment-keeper) of the first-rank temple at Soknopaiou Nesos. Filed an administrative declaration on 7 April 216 CE regarding property holdings. Representative of the priestly class that administered the oracle services of the Fayum temples.

achilles-son-of-nearchidas-also-called-c.canonical_name: Achilles son of Nearchidas also called Cornelius
achilles-son-of-nearchidas-also-called-c.bio: Hermopolitan notable pressed into the expensive office of cosmetes in a council meeting whose minutes survive almost verbatim.
achilles-son-of-nearchidas-also-called-c.disambiguation: Distinct from the later Achilles son of Polydeuces mentioned in P.Ryl. 2.181.

nearchidas-also-called-cornelius-hermopo.bio: Achilles' father, remembered in the council hall as "the lover of office, the brave old man."
nearchidas-also-called-cornelius-hermopo.disambiguation: Probably descended from the earlier Hermopolitan Nearchas son of Eudaimon, but that identification remains inferential.

diogenes-son-of-hermaios-hermopolis-magn.bio: Former cosmetes who helped block Achilles' attempted move into the exegeteia and remained tied to municipal grain and festival business for years.
diogenes-son-of-hermaios-hermopolis-magn.disambiguation: Identified through the same patronymic across multiple Hermopolitan documents.

olympiodorus-hermopolis-magna-active-192.bio: Lawyer at the council meeting who turned Achilles' own counteroffer against him.
olympiodorus-hermopolis-magna-active-192.disambiguation: Known here only from the council minutes.

aspidas-father-of-hermas-hermopolis-magn.bio: Former cosmetes who guaranteed Achilles' nomination "on my personal responsibility."
aspidas-father-of-hermas-hermopolis-magn.disambiguation: Not securely identifiable with the much earlier Aspidas attested in Oxyrhynchus.

ammonion-son-of-dioscorus-hermopolis-mag.bio: Man who interrupted the meeting to accuse Achilles of assault earlier the same day.
ammonion-son-of-dioscorus-hermopolis-mag.disambiguation: Known here only from this interruption in the council minutes.

sarapion-also-called-apollonius-hermopol.bio: Strategus who supervised the proceedings and had the accusation and nomination recorded.
sarapion-also-called-apollonius-hermopol.disambiguation: Identified by double name in P.Ryl. 2.77.

kametis-son-of-psenchonsis-thebes-active.bio: The fiscal center of this reconstruction, visible through repeated grain, market, and quarter-account entries rather than through narrative documents.
kametis-son-of-psenchonsis-thebes-active.disambiguation: Later Kametis entries may refer to collateral kin or namesakes; the story treats them as part of a household or lineage cluster unless identity is secure.

psenchonsis-thebes-active-105--118-ce.bio: Earlier member of the same fiscal household whose granary appearances anchor the lineage before Kametis.
psenchonsis-thebes-active-105--118-ce.disambiguation: Theban onomastics are repetitive; treated here as the most plausible father-generation anchor.

senchonsis-daughter-of-kametis-thebes-ac.bio: Woman whose repeated appearance in granary and market records shows female-held shares moving through the same household network.
senchonsis-daughter-of-kametis-thebes-ac.disambiguation: Likely linked to Kametis' immediate household, though the precise kin position cannot be proved in every entry.

thaesis-daughter-of-kametis-thebes-activ.bio: Another woman in the Kametis cluster whose appearances suggest the long persistence of female fiscal identity in Theban records.
thaesis-daughter-of-kametis-thebes-activ.disambiguation: Some later Thaesis references may belong to a related branch rather than the same individual.

apollophanes-oxyrhynchus-fl-ca-41-54-ce.bio: Former collector of the trade tax upon weavers. Systematically extorted weavers including Sarapion son of Theon and Alexandrus son of Apollonius.

alexandrus-son-of-apollonius-oxyrhynchus.location: Oxyrhynchus, quarter of the Square of Thoëris
alexandrus-son-of-apollonius-oxyrhynchus.bio: Weaver of Oxyrhynchus. Petitioned about extortion of 16 drachmae by Apollophanes.

tiberius-claudius-pasion-oxyrhynchus-fl-.bio: Strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome during the reign of Claudius. Received petitions from weavers and others.

demetrous-oxyrhynchus-fl-29-37-ce.bio: First wife of Tryphon. Left him and took property, prompting his petition. Mother of Thoönis, who later disappeared.

saraeus-oxyrhynchus-fl-37-66-ce.bio: Second wife of Tryphon. Brought a dowry of 72 drachmae. Mother of Thoenis, apprenticed as a weaver in 66 CE. Marriage contract included hemiolion penalty.

thamounion-oxyrhynchus-fl-44-ce.bio: Mother of Tryphon. In 44 CE reported the disappearance of her grandson Thoönis, requesting his registration as removed for tax purposes.

ptolemaeus-son-of-pausirion-oxyrhynchus-.bio: Master weaver who took Tryphon's son Thoenis as an apprentice in 66 CE.

thaesis-oxyrhynchus-fl-96-ce.bio: Divorced wife of Petosarapis. Received 400 silver drachmae in dowry return during an amicable divorce in 96 CE. Acted with her step-father Onnophris as guardian.

petosarapis-oxyrhynchus-fl-96-ce.patronymic: son of Thompekusis son of Sarapion
petosarapis-oxyrhynchus-fl-96-ce.bio: Former husband of Thaesis. Returned her 400-drachma dowry and parapherna in an amicable divorce in 96 CE.

dionysia-oxyrhynchus-fl-2nd-century-ce.bio: Woman who fought a landmark legal battle against her father when he attempted to dissolve her marriage and reclaim her dowry. The case established precedents affirming women's marriage autonomy.

flavius-titianus-alexandria-fl-2nd-centu.bio: Former prefect whose ruling established that "the decision depends upon the question, with whom the wife wishes to live."

aurelius-gaius-harpocration-alexandria-f.bio: Priest and archidikastes in 267 CE. Used the same title formula as Heraclides two centuries earlier.

ammonarion-oxyrhynchus-fl-after-57-ce.bio: Widow who settled her late husband's 800-drachma dowry obligation through the archidikastes Theon.

julia-heracla-oxyrhynchus-fl-95-ce.bio: Roman citizen woman who transferred five arourae of land to her daughter in 95 CE, acting with a praefect-assigned guardian.

sieapis-oxyrhynchus-fl-ca-76-ce.patronymic: daughter of Sarapion son of Apollonius
sieapis-oxyrhynchus-fl-ca-76-ce.bio: Woman who registered property in her own name around 76 CE, using her son Ariston as guardian.

senyris-oxyrhynchus-fl-ca-156-125-bce.bio: Woman who petitioned the chief judge after her partner threw her out, stopped feeding and clothing her and their two children, and took another woman.

diemous-oxyrhynchus-fl-144-ce.bio: Woman who reported a burglary, naming her neighbor Heras the weaver as suspect.

serenus-oxyrhynchus-fl-2nd-c-ce.bio: Husband (and possibly brother) of Isidora. After she left, he did not bathe for a month, wept by night, and wrote desperate letters through intermediaries. Accused of selling Isidora's gold chain and putting her on a boat.

colobus-oxyrhynchus-fl-2nd-c-ce.bio: Intermediary between Serenus and Isidora. Conveyed accusations in both directions: told Serenus that Isidora accused him of selling her chain; Isidora accused Colobus of "making her a prostitute."

panetbeous-theadelphia-arsinoite-nome-fl.bio: Public farmer from Theadelphia who petitioned the centurion Claudius Alexandros after his cohabiting wife left with household goods and was subsequently seized and married by Neilos son of Syros.

wife-of-panetbeous-theadelphia-arsinoite.bio: Unnamed cohabiting wife of Panetbeous, mother of his children. Left his house during his absence, taking household goods including a white garment, an Oxyrhynchite pillow, a cross-striped double-blanket, materials for two tunics, and agricultural tools. Refused to return property despite repeated demands. Subsequently joined Neilos son of Syros.

neilos-theadelphia-arsinoite-nome-fl-mid.bio: Man from Theadelphia who "seized and married" the wife of Panetbeous. Accused by Panetbeous of unlawful seizure.

demetria-elephantine-fl-310-bce.bio: Koan woman who married Herakleides the Temnitan in 310 BCE under the earliest known complete Greek marriage contract from Egypt. The contract protected her against her husband's infidelity with a double penalty.

saraeus-oxyrhynchus-fl-37-ce.bio: Second wife of the weaver Tryphon. Brought a modest dowry of 72 drachmae (cash, gold earrings, and a milk-white robe) to a marriage that existed without a formal contract.

aurelia-tausiris-oxyrhynchus-fl-260-ce.bio: Bride whose mother gave her in marriage with a lavish dowry of gold jewelry (necklace, brooch, pearl earrings, ring) and Dalmatian veils.

aurelia-aristous-oxyrhynchus-fl-mid-3rd-.patronymic: daughter of Aurelius Herodes son of Apion
aurelia-aristous-oxyrhynchus-fl-mid-3rd-.bio: Daughter of the ex-gymnasiarch and senator Herodes. Claimed a dowry of 2 talents and 3,000 drachmae from her deceased husband Achillion's estate, valued at 200,000 sesterces.

kronous-philoumene-arsinoite-nome-fl-113.patronymic: daughter of Kastor son of Pyrion
kronous-philoumene-arsinoite-nome-fl-113.bio: Twenty-year-old woman who divorced her husband Lysimachos in an orderly legal proceeding, receiving back her dowry and the right to marry again. Had a son named Herakleides also called Valerius.

thenstotoetis-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl.patronymic: daughter of Heron son of Neilos
thenstotoetis-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl.bio: Sixteen-year-old woman who divorced her husband Lysas, a freedman, after a marriage that lasted approximately two years. Received back a 300-drachma dowry. Free to marry again without accusation.

zois-alexandria-fl-augustan-period.bio: Woman who separated amicably from Antipatros, receiving back garments valued at 120 drachmae and a pair of gold earrings. Both parties declared free to remarry.

makaria-unknown-egypt-fl-after-55-bce.bio: Woman whose husband threw her out of the house, did not provide necessities or clothing, and did not return her dowry.

philumene-oxyrhynchus-fl-82-ce.bio: Woman who was "continually pressing" for repayment of 2,000 drachmae.

kronous-also-called-philoumene-tebtunis-.patronymic: daughter of Kastor son of Pyrion
kronous-also-called-philoumene-tebtunis-.bio: Twenty-year-old woman whose divorce deed in Tebtunis preserves the clause that she may marry again "the man whom she prefers." Her father received back her dowry in gold, silver, and paraphernalia.

lysimachos-former-husband-of-kronous-teb.bio: Former husband of Kronous also called Philoumene. In the divorce deed he acknowledges the dissolution of the marriage and the return of the dowry to Kronous's father.
