lysimachos-former-husband-of-kronous-teb.disambiguation: Not the Lysimachos brother in P.Fam.Tebt. 21, unless later family continuity is assumed.

kastor-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-113-11.bio: Father and legal guardian of Kronous in her divorce deed. He receives the returned dowry in gold, silver, and paraphernalia.

herakleides-also-called-valerius-tebtuni.patronymic: son of Herakleides son of Maron
herakleides-also-called-valerius-tebtuni.bio: One of the brothers who paid the remaining marriage money owed to Didymarion. He later appears assisting Didyme in a land cession. He may plausibly be identical with the son named in Kronous's divorce deed, though that identification is not certain.
herakleides-also-called-valerius-tebtuni.disambiguation: Possible but unproven identification with the child in P.Fam.Tebt. 13.
physical_ids: scar on right or left knee depending on document

didymarion-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-12.patronymic: daughter of Herakleides son of Maron
didymarion-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-12.bio: Woman who acknowledged receipt from her brothers of money long owed from her father's marriage promises, along with additional gifts and property guarantees from her mother.

didyme-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-122-12.patronymic: daughter of Lysimachos son of Lysimachos
didyme-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-122-12.bio: Elderly mother in the Tebtunis family archive who added jewelry, clothing, and housing rights to her daughter's marriage portion and later ceded land near Ibion Eikosipentarouron.

apia-also-called-herakleia-arsinoite-nom.patronymic: daughter of Herakleia also called Isidora and the late Kronion
apia-also-called-herakleia-arsinoite-nom.bio: Woman who lived in an unwritten marriage with Herakleides son of Sarapion, became pregnant, gave birth, and died, leaving behind a contested infant and a family dispute.

herakleia-also-called-isidora-arsinoite-.bio: Mother of Apia also called Herakleia. She and her kin challenged the paternal claim to the infant born from Apia's unwritten marriage.

lysas-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-123-ce.patronymic: freedman of Herakleia daughter of Lysimachos
lysas-tebtunis-arsinoite-nome-fl-123-ce.bio: Freedman whose marriage to Thenstotoetis was formally annulled. The deed required return of the dowry and barred future accusation.

isidora-egypt-roman-period.bio: Woman who entered a wet-nursing contract with Didyma, entrusting an infant to her care for sixteen months under close bodily and domestic restrictions.
isidora-egypt-roman-period.disambiguation: A common name; specific family details are not preserved in the short translation used here.

didyma-egypt-roman-period.bio: Woman hired to nurse Isidora's infant in her own home. The contract governing her labor also regulated sex, pregnancy, and the exclusivity of her milk.

sieapis-cercemunis-egypt-fl-76-ce.patronymic: daughter of Sarapion son of Apollonios
sieapis-cercemunis-egypt-fl-76-ce.bio: Woman who registered a house in Cercemunis as belonging jointly to herself and her sister Thais.

thais-cercemunis-egypt-fl-76-ce.bio: Sister of Sieapis and co-holder with her of a house recorded in a property return from Cercemunis.

pathermouthis-oxyrhynchus-region-egypt-l.bio: Woman who wrote to "my sister, mistress Dionysia" asking her to carry out registration on behalf of herself and Patas.

dionysia-oxyrhynchus-region-egypt-late-3.bio: Addressed as "my sister, mistress Dionysia" in a letter asking her to handle official registration for absent kin or intimates.

eirene-egypt-4th-century-ce.bio: Woman who wrote to her sister Asphaleia with greetings, family news, and a request to buy cumin.

asphaleia-egypt-4th-century-ce.bio: Sister of Eirene and the recipient of a small but intimate domestic letter.

aurelios-pakysis-soknopaiou-nesos-arsino.bio: Priest and vestment-keeper of the first-rank temple at Soknopaiou Nesos. His declaration shows that temple office was embedded in local property, status, and administration.
aurelios-pakysis-soknopaiou-nesos-arsino.disambiguation: Not identical with other priests named Pakysis without the same patronymic.

roles.title: priest and warden of the temple of Isis

poregebthis-berenikis-thesmophorou-arsin.bio: Priest and temple warden whose assault petition preserves one of the best snapshots of portable temple wealth: honey, valuable linen, bronze equipment, cash, and vessels.

patumis-egypt-mid-3rd-century-bce.bio: Steward of Isis who treated the cows of Isis and Osiris as managed productive assets from which he expected to derive a living.

roles.title: lamplighter of the temple of Sarapis

thonis-also-called-patoiphis-oxyrhynchus.bio: One of the four lamplighters who swore to maintain temple lamps and supply proper oil for the shrines of Sarapis, Isis, and Thoeris.

apollonius-oxyrhynchite-upper-toparchy-f.bio: Village secretary responsible for an account of tax abatements, including sacred land belonging to Isis of Taposiris Aquila.

neoteros-karanis-or-the-fayum-fl-21-22-c.bio: Asked Soxis Pnepheros whether he should go to Alexandria to bring a complaint. His surviving ticket preserves the decision before it was made.

asklepiades-fayum-fl-6-ce.bio: Asked Soknopaios whether he was permitted to marry Tapetheus daughter of Marres.

tapetheus-fayum-fl-6-ce.bio: The woman Asklepiades hoped to marry; the oracle ticket notes that she had previously been the wife of Horion.

roles.title: woman named in Christian oracle question

theodora-egypt-501-700-ce.bio: Named in a Christian oracle question asking whether she should be given to Joseph.

roles.title: prospective husband in Christian oracle question

joseph-egypt-501-700-ce.bio: Named as the proposed husband in a later Christian oracle question concerning Theodora.

aurelius-euripas-euergetis-fl-late-3rd-c.bio: Actor summoned by civic officials of Euergetis to perform at the birthday festival of Cronus.

aurelius-sarapas-euergetis-fl-late-3rd-c.bio: Homeric reciter summoned with the actor Euripas for the festival of Cronus.

aurelius-cornelianus-oxyrhynchus-fl-late.bio: Leader of a troupe engaged by village presidents of Sinkepha to entertain at local festivities.
aurelius-cornelianus-oxyrhynchus-fl-late.disambiguation: Patronymic partially uncertain in the damaged text.

petosiris-oxyrhynchus-3rd-4th-century-ce.bio: Writer of a practical invitation asking how transport should be arranged for a guest coming to a divine birthday festival.

epaphroditus-senepta-d-182-ce.bio: Child of about eight who died after leaning out to watch castanet- players performing during a festival.

dionysus-son-of-apollodorus-son-of-diony.canonical_name: Dionysus son of Apollodorus son of Dionysius
dionysus-son-of-apollodorus-son-of-diony.patronymic: son of Apollodorus son of Dionysius
dionysus-son-of-apollodorus-son-of-diony.bio: Public physician of Oxyrhynchus who inspected the body of the hanged man Hierax and reported his findings to the strategus.

heron-oxyrhynchus-fl-316-ce.bio: One of two public physicians who inspected a fever case under official order and reported their findings to the logistes.

didymus-son-of-dioscorus-oxyrhynchus-fl-.bio: Public physician of Oxyrhynchus who joined Heron in inspecting a man with fever and reporting the case officially.

demetrios-oxyrhynchus-fl-246-bce.bio: Physician whose wage was paid through the bank from the account of the physician-tax.

eudaimon-egypt-4th-century-ce.bio: Physician whose surviving family letter shows the domestic side of medical life and concern for absent relatives.

sarapion-son-of-artemidorus-son-of-ptole.canonical_name: Sarapion son of Artemidorus son of Ptolemaeus
sarapion-son-of-artemidorus-son-of-ptole.patronymic: son of Artemidorus son of Ptolemaeus

roles.title: lessee of perfume and unguent business

sarapion-son-of-artemidorus-son-of-ptole.bio: Man who sought to lease a share in a perfume-selling and unguent-making business for monthly payment.

castor-son-of-antiphilus-ptolemais-euerg.bio: Holder of a commercial share in the perfume and unguent trade which he leased out to Sarapion.

achillis-oxyrhynchus-late-3rd-century-ce.bio: Recipient of a shopping order for perfume, powder, combs, oil, and other mixed household goods.

roles.title: camel-driver linked to barber's equipment transfer

kalokeros-theadelphia-fl-249-268-ce.bio: Camel-driver named in the transfer of timber intended for barber's equipment.

aurelius-agathus-euergetis-late-3rd-cent.bio: Co-signatory of the performer summons in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-hermanoubammon-euergetis-late-3.bio: Co-signatory of the performer summons in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-didymus-euergetis-late-3rd-cent.bio: Co-signatory of the performer summons in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-coprias-euergetis-late-3rd-cent.bio: Co-signatory of the performer summons in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-euripas-euergetis-late-3rd-cent.bio: Named performer summoned to the birthday festival of Cronus in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-sarapas-euergetis-late-3rd-cent.bio: Named performer summoned alongside Euripas in P.Oxy. 7.1025.

aurelius-cornelianus-oxyrhynchus-late-3r.bio: Addressee of the village hiring letter in P.Oxy. 74.5016.

dioscurides-oxyrhynchus-323-ce.bio: Announces the ephebic display before spectators in P.Oxy. 1.42.

petosiris-oxyrhynchus-region-3rd-4th-cen.bio: Invites Serenia to the god's birthday celebration in P.Oxy. 1.112.

serenia-oxyrhynchus-region-3rd-4th-centu.bio: Asked whether she will travel by boat or donkey to attend the festival in P.Oxy. 1.112.

epaphroditus-senepta-182-ce.bio: Died after leaning out to watch festival performers in P.Oxy. 3.475.

plution-senepta-182-ce.bio: His house was the site of the performance described in P.Oxy. 3.475.

dionysus-oxyrhynchus-173-ce.bio: Inspected the body of Hierax and reported to the strategus.

heron-oxyrhynchus-316-ce.bio: One of the two doctors who certified a fever case for the logistes.

didymus-oxyrhynchus-316-ce.bio: Co-author of the fever inspection report.

demetrios-egypt-246-bce.bio: Received pay from the account of the physician-fund.

athenagoras-the-labyrinth-around-101-bce.bio: Attached to the priests of the stolistai in the Labyrinth.

artemidorus-egypt-3rd-century-bce.bio: Appears in crop and account texts with grain, sesame, and poppy seed.

sarapion-ptolemais-euergetis-161-ce.bio: Sought to lease part of a perfume-selling and scent-making business.

castor-ptolemais-euergetis-161-ce.bio: Lessor in the contract for the perfume-selling business.

petesis-egypt-3rd-century-bce.bio: Named as the perfume seller in a Zenon letter.

roles.title: camel-driver attached to barber equipment transfer

kalokeros-theadelphia-249-268-ce.bio: Mentioned in the letter carrying wood for barber's equipment.

hermione-oxyrhynchus-131-ce.bio: Woman at the center of the poisoning accusation and mother of Dionysia.

dionysia-oxyrhynchus-and-alexandria-afte.bio: Daughter of Hermione who petitioned against Sarapion and defended her purchase of vineyard and grain land.

sarapion-oxyrhynchus-131-ce.bio: Man who said he had been poisoned after visiting Hermione and later disputed property with Dionysia.

chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Father of Dionysia and plausible participant in the larger family-property dossier.
chaeremon-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.disambiguation: Treated cautiously as the same Chaeremon across the linked dossier because the damaged text leaves room for uncertainty.

horion-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Dionysia's husband in the larger family dossier.

roles.title: father of Sarapion and earlier seller

mnesitheus-oxyrhynchus-2nd-century-ce.bio: Father of Sarapion and earlier seller of the disputed property.

smaragdus-oxyrhynchus-131-ce.bio: Missing slave mentioned in the argument surrounding the accusation.

alexandria-lower-egypt.name: Alexandria
alexandria-lower-egypt.bio: Capital of Roman Egypt and seat of the praefect. Dionysia traveled roughly 200 km from Oxyrhynchus to Alexandria in 131 CE to appear before the praefect's court. The city was the ultimate destination for legal disputes that provincial officials could not resolve.

oxyrhynchus-oxyrhynchite-nome-heptanomis.name: Oxyrhynchus
oxyrhynchus-oxyrhynchite-nome-heptanomis.bio: Third-largest city in Roman Egypt and capital of the Oxyrhynchite nome. One of the most important archaeological sites for ancient papyri. In the second century CE it was dominated by wealthy families who held gymnasiarchies and other liturgical offices. Its record offices (bibliotheke enkteseon), courts, temples, and gymnasium formed the institutional backdrop of Dionysia's legal battles.

temgenouthis-oxyrhynchus.name: Temgenouthis
temgenouthis-oxyrhynchus.bio: A quarter (amphodon) of Oxyrhynchus where Dionysia owned a house with courts, two yards (one with a well), and a portico, which she leased to Ptolema of Antinoë in 164 CE for 200 silver drachmas per year.

paberke-oxyrhynchite-nome-eastern-toparc.name: Paberke
paberke-oxyrhynchite-nome-eastern-toparc.bio: Village in the eastern toparchy of the Oxyrhynchite nome, home of Psenamounis, Dionysia's tenant farmer who leased her 38 arourae beginning in 142 CE.

antinoë-antinoite-nome-heptanomis-roman-.name: Antinoë
antinoë-antinoite-nome-heptanomis-roman-.bio: City founded by Hadrian in 130 CE in honor of his companion Antinous. The house Dionysia leased in 164 CE had previously belonged to her second cousin Chaeremon of Antinoë, and her tenant Ptolema was from this city.

theadelphia-arsinoite-nome-themistos-div.name: Theadelphia
theadelphia-arsinoite-nome-themistos-div.bio: Medium-sized village in the Fayum depression, founded in the early Ptolemaic period and named for Arsinoe II Philadelphos. Center of the largest division of the Appianus estate, with c. 250 arourai of arable land and c. 100 arourai of vineyards. Had its own granary, bath-house, and administrative offices. The Heroneinos archive of approximately 1,000 papyri was excavated here by Grenfell and Hunt.

alexandria-coastal-egypt.name: Alexandria
alexandria-coastal-egypt.bio: Residence of the estate owner Aurelius Appianus. Destination for wine and grain shipments from the Fayum estates.

arsinoe-arsinoite-nome.name: Arsinoe
arsinoe-arsinoite-nome.bio: Capital of the Arsinoite nome. Administrative seat of the Appianus estate's central administration under Alypios.

euhemeria-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divis.name: Euhemeria
euhemeria-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divis.bio: Village managed by the phrontistes Eirenaios as a division of the Appianus estate.

philoteris-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divi.name: Philoteris
philoteris-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divi.bio: Village managed by the phrontistes Palas as a division of the Appianus estate.

dionysias-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divis.name: Dionysias
dionysias-arsinoite-nome-themistos-divis.bio: Village at the western edge of the Fayum managed by Horion.

sathro-arsinoite-nome.name: Sathro
sathro-arsinoite-nome.bio: Estate division with fishing grounds extending six schoinoi along the shore.

taureinos-arsinoite-nome.name: Taureinos
taureinos-arsinoite-nome.bio: Estate division with timber resources. In 266 CE, cattle were transferred there to trample grain being eaten by mice.

ibion-argaiou-arsinoite-nome.name: Ibion Argaiou
ibion-argaiou-arsinoite-nome.bio: Estate division from which 100 jars of wine were released for the Amesysia festival at Theadelphia.

ptolemais-hormou-arsinoite-nome.name: Ptolemais Hormou
ptolemais-hormou-arsinoite-nome.bio: Estate holding where a ten-cubit axle broke and required emergency replacement.

talei-arsinoite-nome.name: Talei
talei-arsinoite-nome.bio: Grape-harvesting location for the Appianus estate.

oxyrhynchus-oxyrhynchite-nome-middle-egy.name: Oxyrhynchus
oxyrhynchus-oxyrhynchite-nome-middle-egy.bio: Major city on the Bahr Yusuf canal in Middle Egypt, capital of the Oxyrhynchite nome. One of the most important administrative and economic centres in Roman Egypt, with a functioning city senate, gymnasium, temples, and extensive bureaucratic apparatus. Called "the illustrious and most illustrious city" in official documents of the Tetrarchic period. The ancient rubbish mounds yielded tens of thousands of papyri when excavated by Grenfell and Hunt beginning in 1897. Techosis filed her petition here in 295 CE before the beneficiarius at his statio.

small-oasis-mikra-oasis-heptanomia-admin.name: Small Oasis (Mikra Oasis)
small-oasis-mikra-oasis-heptanomia-admin.bio: Cluster of settlements in Egypt's Western Desert, approximately 370 km southwest of Cairo. Known as Oasis parva in Latin from 45 CE. Administratively linked to the Oxyrhynchite nome and grouped with the Heptanomia under the epistrategos. Garrisoned by Roman troops supplied from Oxyrhynchus, with a military fort at Qarat el-Toub. Connected to the Nile valley by desert roads monitored by Roman fortresses. Home community of Aurelia Techosis and her mother before they migrated to Oxyrhynchus.

karanis-arsinoite-nome-fayum.name: Karanis
karanis-arsinoite-nome-fayum.bio: Village in the Fayum region from which Aurelia Taesis filed a petition to a beneficiarius in 297 CE, and where Aurelia Herois filed a census declaration in 299 CE.

teruthis-oxyrhynchite-nome.name: Teruthis
teruthis-oxyrhynchite-nome.bio: Village whose comarchs received orders from a beneficiarius regarding the transfer of a prisoner named Pachoumis.

paimis-oxyrhynchite-nome-western-toparch.name: Paimis
paimis-oxyrhynchite-nome-western-toparch.bio: Village in the western toparchy of the Oxyrhynchite nome whose inhabitants petitioned the deputy praefect in 298 CE about administrative difficulties under the Tetrarchy.

oxyrhynchus.name: Oxyrhynchus
oxyrhynchus.bio: Capital of the Oxyrhynchite nome in Middle Egypt, on the western bank of the Bahr Yusuf canal, approximately 160 km south-southwest of Cairo. One of the most important archaeological sites for Greek papyri, excavated from 1896 by Grenfell and Hunt, yielding tens of thousands of documents spanning a millennium of daily life. Euphrosyne lived, was enslaved, and was freed here. Economy based on agriculture (irrigated wheat and flax) and textile production.

vaughan-library-harrow-school.name: Vaughan Library, Harrow School
vaughan-library-harrow-school.bio: Repository of P.Oxy. 1.48, Euphrosyne's manumission document. Part of the distribution of Oxyrhynchus papyri to institutions that funded the excavations by Grenfell and Hunt.

seryphis.name: Seryphis
seryphis.bio: Village in the Oxyrhynchite nome where the freedwoman Syntyche received grain transfers in 135 CE (P.Oxy. 72.4875, corpus ID 42635).

small-oasis-psobthis.name: Small Oasis (Psobthis)
small-oasis-psobthis.bio: Settlement in the Western Desert where Sarapias daughter of Podon lived, the debtor of the freedman Serenus. Shows the geographic reach of Oxyrhynchite economic networks.

delphi.name: Delphi
delphi.bio: Panhellenic sanctuary of Apollo in central Greece where over 1,000 manumission inscriptions survive, documenting the sacral manumission system in which slaves were fictitiously "sold" to the god. The Zeus-Earth-Sun formula used in Egypt served an analogous function.

this-thinis-upper-egypt-thinite-nome-the.name: This (Thinis)
this-thinis-upper-egypt-thinite-nome-the.region: Upper Egypt, Thinite nome, Theban province
this-thinis-upper-egypt-thinite-nome-the.bio: One of the most ancient cities in Egypt, traditionally considered the first capital of united Egypt under Menes (c. 3100 BCE). By the Byzantine period it functioned as a village (kome). Its exact site remains unidentified; scholarly consensus places it near Abydos and modern Girga.

panopolis-akhmim-upper-egypt-panopolite-.name: Panopolis (Akhmim)
panopolis-akhmim-upper-egypt-panopolite-.bio: Major textile production centre in Upper Egypt. Home city of Psates and Maria before they migrated to This. Site of the family's secondary property holdings on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos. Strabo mentions its linen-weaving. Archaeological excavations have produced elaborately woven textiles from the 5th-7th centuries CE.

aphrodite-aphrodito-upper-egypt-antaiopo.name: Aphrodite (Aphrodito)
aphrodite-aphrodito-upper-egypt-antaiopo.bio: Village in Upper Egypt with an exceptionally rich papyrological archive. The 615 CE house sale by Eudoxia provides a comparandum for the family's property transactions.

antinoopolis-middle-egypt.name: Antinoopolis
antinoopolis-middle-egypt.modern_location: near Sheikh Ibada, Minya Governorate, Egypt
antinoopolis-middle-egypt.bio: City founded by the emperor Hadrian in 130 CE to honour his drowned beloved Antinous. Its cemetery yielded the most complete archaeological assemblage of love magic: the clay figurine pierced with thirteen needles, the inscribed lead tablet, and the clay pot in which both were deposited, following the instructions of PGM IV.296-466.

vineyard-of-lynochois-upper-egypt-thinit.name: Vineyard of Lynochois
vineyard-of-lynochois-upper-egypt-thinit.bio: Vineyard near the village of This where Kallinikos was to deliver wine to Pachymios in repayment of his 606 CE loan.

gela.name: Gela
gela.modern_location: Gela, Province of Caltanissetta, Sicily, Italy
gela.bio: Greek colony on the southern coast of Sicily, founded c. 689 BCE by colonists from Rhodes and Crete. One of the most powerful cities in the Greek west during the early 5th century BCE, under the tyrants Hippocrates and Gelon. Findspot of Apellis's curse tablet and several sympotic inscriptions. Aeschylus died and was buried here in 456/455 BCE. The city was diminished after 485 BCE when Gelon transferred half its population to Syracuse, but remained a significant cultural center throughout the 5th century.

selinus-selinunte.name: Selinus (Selinunte)
selinus-selinunte.modern_location: Castelvetrano, Province of Trapani, Sicily, Italy
selinus-selinunte.bio: The westernmost Greek colony in Sicily, founded c. 651 or 628 BCE by colonists from Megara Hyblaea. Site of the earliest known corpus of Greek curse tablets (defixiones), dating to the late 6th and early 5th centuries BCE. Also the source of the great victory inscription (ISic001087) listing nine divine patrons. Destroyed by Carthage in 409 BCE. Its sanctuary of Demeter Malophoros was a major cult site where some curse tablets were deposited.

syracuse.name: Syracuse
syracuse.bio: The most powerful Greek city in Sicily, originally founded c. 734 BCE by Corinthian colonists. Became the dominant city of the Greek west after Gelon transferred the populations of Gela and other cities there in 485 BCE. Capital of the Deinomenid tyranny and a major center of Greek culture in the western Mediterranean.

akragas-agrigento.name: Akragas (Agrigento)
akragas-agrigento.bio: Greek colony in south-central Sicily, founded c. 580 BCE as a colony of Gela. One of the three Sicilian cities (with Selinus and Gela) that produced the earliest known Greek curse tablets, dating to the late 6th and early 5th centuries BCE.

oxyrhynchus-middle-egypt.name: Oxyrhynchus
oxyrhynchus-middle-egypt.bio: Major city in Middle Egypt and one of the richest papyrological sites in the world. Source of the Arian amulet (P.Oxy. inv. 2065, corpus ID 17372), the Joannia amulet (P.Oxy. 8.1151, corpus ID 22951), and numerous other magical, medical, and documentary texts. The city's papyrus dumps, excavated from 1896 onward by Grenfell and Hunt, have yielded over 500,000 fragments.

hawara-faiyum-depression.name: Hawara
hawara-faiyum-depression.bio: Cemetery site in the Faiyum where love spells and magical texts have been found. Known for its Roman-era mummy portraits and the extensive necropolis that made it a natural site for the deposition of grave magic.

fayum-arsinoite-nome-faiyum-depression-m.name: Fayum (Arsinoite nome)
fayum-arsinoite-nome-faiyum-depression-m.bio: Fertile depression southwest of Cairo, one of the most densely documented regions of Roman Egypt. Many magical texts, love spells, and amulets derive from the Faiyum's cemeteries and settlements.

ionia-western-anatolia.name: Ionia
ionia-western-anatolia.bio: The Greek-settled coast of western Anatolia. After the Ionian Revolt (499-494 BCE), Persian forces crushed the Greek cities and systematically castrated the most handsome boys for service as eunuchs. The site of the earliest documented large-scale forced castration in Greek sources.

pedasa-caria-southwestern-anatolia.name: Pedasa
pedasa-caria-southwestern-anatolia.bio: A small town in Caria, near Halicarnassus, birthplace of Hermotimus. Herodotus notes that the people of Pedasa had a peculiar omen: whenever danger threatened, the priestess of Athena grew a great beard.

chios-eastern-aegean.name: Chios
chios-eastern-aegean.bio: Aegean island, home of the slave-trader Panionius who made his living castrating beautiful boys and selling them to the Persian market.

sardis-lydia-western-anatolia.name: Sardis
sardis-lydia-western-anatolia.bio: Capital of Lydia and major Persian satrapal seat. One of the primary markets where castrated boys were sold. Xerxes used Sardis as a staging point for his invasion of Greece in 480 BCE. Site of the encounter between Hermotimus and Panionius.

pessinus-galatia-central-anatolia.name: Pessinus
pessinus-galatia-central-anatolia.bio: Centre of the cult of Cybele and Attis. The Gauls (Galatians) who settled in the region maintained the cult and abstained from pork, following the myth that Attis was killed by a boar. Pausanias records that the Gauls of Pessinus maintained the prohibition.

hierapolis-syrian-northern-syria.name: Hierapolis (Syrian)
hierapolis-syrian-northern-syria.bio: Site of the great temple of the Syrian Goddess (Atargatis) described by Lucian of Samosata. Home of the Galli priesthood whose self-castration rituals Lucian describes in detail. According to Lucian, the legendary Combabos was the founding figure of the Galli at Hierapolis.

rome-palatine-hill-latium.name: Rome (Palatine Hill)
rome-palatine-hill-latium.bio: Site of the Temple of Magna Mater (Cybele), established in 204 BCE after the Romans officially adopted the cult during the Second Punic War. The annual festivals of Attis, including the Dies Sanguinis on March 24, were celebrated here.

attica-central-greece.name: Attica
attica-central-greece.bio: Region surrounding Athens. Source of the tomb curses IG II^2 13209 and 13210 (corpus IDs 75579, 75580) with their disease lists invoking shivering, fever, quartan ague, and elephantiasis.

cos-kos-eastern-aegean.name: Cos (Kos)
cos-kos-eastern-aegean.bio: Island associated with the Hippocratic medical school. Traditional birthplace of Hippocrates and probable origin of the treatise On the Sacred Disease (corpus ID 134569).

egypt-post-conquest-north-africa-eastern.name: Egypt (post-conquest)
egypt-post-conquest-north-africa-eastern.bio: After the Arab conquest of 641 CE, Egypt retained its Coptic-speaking Christian majority for centuries while gradually arabicising. The healing amulet for Ahmed son of Mariam (corpus ID 6331) demonstrates the persistence of Coptic magical practice serving Muslim clients in the post-conquest period.

karanis-fayum-arsinoite-nome-lower-egypt.name: Karanis
karanis-fayum-arsinoite-nome-lower-egypt.bio: Village in the northeastern Fayum, excavated extensively by the University of Michigan (1924-1935). Home to two temples dedicated to the crocodile gods Pnepheros and Petesouchos. Probable site where Neoteros submitted his oracle question to Soxis Pnepheros in 21-22 CE. The Michigan excavations recovered houses, granaries, temples, and thousands of papyri and artifacts documenting village life over centuries.

soknopaiou-nesos-fayum-arsinoite-nome-lo.name: Soknopaiou Nesos
soknopaiou-nesos-fayum-arsinoite-nome-lo.bio: Village on the northern shore of Lake Moeris (Birket Qarun) in the Fayum, dominated by the temple of the crocodile god Soknopaios. The single most important source of Greek oracle questions from Egypt. Asklepiades submitted his marriage oracle question here in 6 CE. The village was gradually abandoned by the mid-third century CE.

krokodilopolis-arsinoe-fayum-arsinoite-n.name: Krokodilopolis (Arsinoe)
krokodilopolis-arsinoe-fayum-arsinoite-n.bio: Capital of the Arsinoite nome (Fayum). Originally named "City of Crocodiles" after the crocodile cult. Renamed Arsinoe under the Ptolemies. Site where the Christian oracle question about Theodora and Joseph was found (501-700 CE).

dios-praesidium-eastern-desert-upper-egy.name: Dios (praesidium)
dios-praesidium-eastern-desert-upper-egy.modern_location: Eastern Desert of Egypt, between Coptos and Berenike
dios-praesidium-eastern-desert-upper-egy.bio: Roman military fortlet on the road from Coptos to the Red Sea port of Berenike. Excavated by Helene Cuvigny. Contained a shrine dedicated to Zeus Helios Megas Sarapis with lot oracle texts on ostraca dating to 175-225 CE. The oracles resemble the astragaloi traditions of southwestern Asia Minor.

xeron-pelagos-praesidium-eastern-desert-.name: Xeron Pelagos (praesidium)
xeron-pelagos-praesidium-eastern-desert-.modern_location: Eastern Desert of Egypt, between Coptos and Berenike
xeron-pelagos-praesidium-eastern-desert-.bio: Roman military fortlet in the Eastern Desert, named "Dry Sea" with characteristic military humour. Site where oracle tickets bearing divine names (Hermes, Ares, a chthonian daimon) were found. Also yielded a postal register of the curator Turbo, documenting the logistics of desert communication.

alexandria-mediterranean-coast-lower-egy.name: Alexandria
alexandria-mediterranean-coast-lower-egy.bio: Capital of Ptolemaic and Roman Egypt, seat of the provincial governor (prefect) and the Roman courts. The destination Neoteros contemplated travelling to in order to bring a legal complaint. A journey of several days from the Fayum by Nile transport.

hermopolis-magna-middle-egypt-hermopolit.name: Hermopolis Magna
hermopolis-magna-middle-egypt-hermopolit.bio: Major city of Middle Egypt whose municipal elite generated the council minutes and related civic documents used in this story.

caesareum-hermopolite-nome.name: Caesareum
caesareum-hermopolite-nome.bio: Temple of the imperial cult that also functioned as a civic meeting place.

skordon-upper-patemite-district.name: Skordon
skordon-upper-patemite-district.bio: Village in the Hermopolite nome where Diogenes and his colleague handled barley distribution.

thebes-upper-egypt-theban-nome.name: Thebes
thebes-upper-egypt-theban-nome.bio: Major Upper Egyptian center whose granaries, quarter accounts, and market lists generated the ostraca that preserve Kametis' world.

charax-theban-nome.name: Charax
charax-theban-nome.bio: One of the quarter designations that recurs in the grain and tax records linked to the Kametis cluster.

agora-theban-nome.name: Agora
agora-theban-nome.bio: Fiscal and market context repeatedly associated with Kametis, Senchonsis, and related names in assessment lists.

tebtunis-polemon-division-arsinoite-nome.name: Tebtunis
tebtunis-polemon-division-arsinoite-nome.bio: Fayum village whose papyri preserve unusually dense documentation of marriage contracts, divorce deeds, family settlements, tax records, and notarial activity, making it one of the best places to study the material history of ordinary families in Roman Egypt.

ibion-eikosipentarouron-arsinoite-nome.name: Ibion Eikosipentarouron
ibion-eikosipentarouron-arsinoite-nome.bio: Site near which Didyme held and ceded catoecic land in 123 CE, showing the extension of Tebtunis family property beyond the village itself.

arsinoite-nome-middle-egypt.name: Arsinoite nome
arsinoite-nome-middle-egypt.bio: Agricultural depression southwest of the Nile valley, one of the most heavily documented regions of the ancient world because of its dense papyrological survival.

cercemunis-oxyrhynchite-or-nearby-admini.name: Cercemunis
cercemunis-oxyrhynchite-or-nearby-admini.bio: Village in which Sieapis filed the return of a house owned jointly with her sister Thais.

soknopaiou-nesos-arsinoite-nome.name: Soknopaiou Nesos
soknopaiou-nesos-arsinoite-nome.bio: Major Fayum temple settlement of the crocodile god Soknopaios. Its documents reveal priestly office, oil-press revenue, sacred food provisioning, and the broader economic life of temple institutions.

nilopolis-arsinoite-sphere.name: Nilopolis
nilopolis-arsinoite-sphere.bio: Site named in the temple account as holding enclosures of Isis and offices tied to Socnopaeus and Enoupis.

karanis-arsinoite-nome.name: Karanis
karanis-arsinoite-nome.bio: Village in the Fayum where temple life, tax paperwork, and ordinary disputes sat close together. Neoteros's oracle ticket belongs to this documentary world.

dios-road-from-coptos-to-berenike.name: Dios
dios-road-from-coptos-to-berenike.bio: Desert praesidium whose shrine preserved oracle ostraca buried in the podium fill.

alexandria-nile-delta.name: Alexandria
alexandria-nile-delta.bio: The imperial metropolis to which Neoteros considered traveling in order to bring his complaint.

euergetis-middle-egypt.name: Euergetis
euergetis-middle-egypt.bio: City from which the civic booking letter to Euripas and Sarapas was sent.

sinkepha-oxyrhynchite-nome.name: Sinkepha
sinkepha-oxyrhynchite-nome.bio: Village whose presidents hired a troupe leader from Oxyrhynchus.

senepta-oxyrhynchite-nome.name: Senepta
senepta-oxyrhynchite-nome.bio: Site of the child's death that required public medical inspection before burial.

theadelphia-arsinoite-nome.name: Theadelphia
theadelphia-arsinoite-nome.bio: Arsinoite village appearing in the gymnasium bath-fuel dossier.

hermopolis-middle-egypt.name: Hermopolis
hermopolis-middle-egypt.bio: Another city where a public physician appears by title in the legal record.

the-labyrinth-arsinoite-nome.name: The Labyrinth
the-labyrinth-arsinoite-nome.bio: Sacred setting for the dossier of the chief physician attached to the stolistai priests.

ptolemais-euergetis-middle-egypt.name: Ptolemais Euergetis
ptolemais-euergetis-middle-egypt.bio: Place of the perfume-business lease in P.Fay. 93.

philadelphia-arsinoite-nome.name: Philadelphia
philadelphia-arsinoite-nome.bio: Arsinoite village where a bathhouse enters the Zenon archive.

oxyrhynchos-middle-egypt.name: Oxyrhynchos
oxyrhynchos-middle-egypt.bio: City where the accusation, family conflict, and property records were rooted.

oxyrhynchite-nome-middle-egypt.name: Oxyrhynchite nome
oxyrhynchite-nome-middle-egypt.bio: Administrative district framing the archive, officials, and land disputes.

archidikastes.title: Archidikastes
archidikastes.description: Highest judicial authority after the Roman prefect. Combined priestly and judicial functions. Supervised the chrematistae courts, managed contract registration and the public records office. Title formula remained stable for centuries. Could compel appearance and order arrest.

strategus.title: Strategus
strategus.description: Chief administrator of an Egyptian nome (district). The archidicastes directed the strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome to serve notice on the debtor in Serenus's case. The strategus oversaw local governance, taxation, and legal enforcement within the nome.
strategus.bio: The strategus Isidorus investigated Chaeremon's property claims and ordered the keepers of the archives to produce their reports confirming Dionysia's rights.

chrematistae.title: Chrematistae
chrematistae.description: Royal judges created by Ptolemy II Philadelphus. Originally itinerant panels of three; became stationary. Eventually absorbed the dikasteria and laokritai courts. Supervised by the archidikastes.

agoranomus.title: Agoranomus
agoranomus.description: Notary public / market magistrate in Roman Egypt. Registered legal documents including sales, mortgages, loans, and manumissions. Multiple agoranomi served simultaneously in larger cities like Oxyrhynchus. The unnamed agoranomus of Oxyrhynchus processed Euphrosyne's manumission on Phaophi 19, Year 6 of Domitian (16 October 86 CE), endorsing it with "Chaeremon, register it" on the same day.

kyrios.title: Kyrios
kyrios.description: Male guardian required for women in most legal transactions. Could be father, husband, brother, son, or appointee. Women with ius liberorum were exempt. Syra notably appears to act without one.

topogrammateus-komogrammateus.title: Topogrammateus / Komogrammateus
topogrammateus-komogrammateus.description: Maintained registrations, tax rolls, and census records. Received reports of missing persons for tax purposes.

praefect-of-egypt.title: Praefect of Egypt
praefect-of-egypt.description: The highest-ranking Roman official in Egypt, an equestrian appointed by the emperor to govern the province. The praefect held supreme judicial and administrative authority, heard appeals from lower officials, issued proclamations affecting the entire province, and made periodic judicial circuits (conventus) through the nomes. Techosis referred to him as "the superior official" (ho meizon) and announced her intention to bring her case before him.
praefect-of-egypt.duties: Supreme judicial and administrative authority in the province. Held annual courts (conventus), heard appeals, issued edicts, and supervised nome governors.
praefect-of-egypt.cost_burden: Imperial appointment; no personal financial burden.
praefect-of-egypt.status_level: Highest provincial authority below the emperor.
praefect-of-egypt.bio: Multiple praefects appear in Dionysia's story: Titus Flavius Titianus (c. 126--133 CE), Valerius Eudaemon (137/8 CE), Longaeus Rufus (c. 185 CE), and Pomponius Faustianus (c. 185--186 CE). Their rulings and precedents formed the legal framework within which Dionysia's battles were fought.

epistrategus.title: Epistrategus
epistrategus.description: Regional administrator serving as intermediary between the praefect and the nome strategoi. Three epistrategoi covered Egypt: the Delta, the Heptanomis (Middle Egypt), and the Thebaid.
epistrategus.duties: Administrative oversight of a region; could serve as delegate judge (iudex pedaneus) when cases were referred by the praefect.
epistrategus.bio: The epistrategoi Claudius Quintianus and Julius Varianus were involved in Dionysia's vineyard case (131 CE). Paconius Felix ruled on the apospasis question in 133/4 CE.

iuridicus.title: Iuridicus
iuridicus.description: A Roman judicial official in Egypt, subordinate to the praefect, with jurisdiction over certain categories of civil cases.
iuridicus.duties: Hearing and deciding legal disputes, particularly those involving status, inheritance, and family law.
iuridicus.bio: The iuridicus Umbrius ruled in 86/7 CE that 'it is worse to take away a wife from her husband than a dowry from a daughter' -- a precedent Dionysia cited a century later.

royal-scribe.title: Royal Scribe
royal-scribe.description: A senior official in the nome administration responsible for record-keeping, tax rolls, and land surveys.
royal-scribe.duties: Maintained official records; could serve as deputy-strategus and render interim judicial decisions.
royal-scribe.bio: Harpocration served as royal scribe and deputy-strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome, presiding over the hearing on June 3, 186 CE in Dionysia's case against her father.

gymnasiarch.title: Gymnasiarch
gymnasiarch.description: The highest civic magistracy in the Greek cities of Roman Egypt. A liturgy requiring substantial personal wealth to fund gymnasium operations.
gymnasiarch.duties: Funding and administration of the gymnasium: oil supply for athletes, maintenance of facilities, organization of festivals and games.
gymnasiarch.cost_burden: Extremely heavy; the gymnasiarch paid for operations out of personal wealth. The office could bankrupt wealthy families.
gymnasiarch.status_level: Highest civic honor in Egyptian metropoleis.
gymnasiarch.bio: Chaeremon son of Phanias, Dionysia's father, was an ex-gymnasiarch. The title marked him as one of the wealthiest and most prominent citizens of Oxyrhynchus, but the financial burden may have contributed to his attempts to reclaim property from his daughter.

eutheniarch.title: Eutheniarch
eutheniarch.description: A civic magistrate responsible for the city's grain supply.
eutheniarch.duties: Overseeing the distribution and supply of grain to the city.
eutheniarch.cost_burden: Liturgical office with personal financial exposure.
eutheniarch.bio: In P.Oxy. 6.908 (199 CE), Apion -- possibly Dionysia's father-in-law or a close relative -- is described as 'ex-gymnasiarch of Oxyrhynchus, at present eutheniarch,' showing the family's continued presence in the highest civic offices.

keeper-of-the-archives.title: Keeper of the Archives
keeper-of-the-archives.description: Officials responsible for maintaining the bibliotheke enkteseon (property registration office) established by Mettius Rufus in 89/90 CE.
keeper-of-the-archives.duties: Registration of property, mortgages, and marriage contracts. Production of reports (prosophonesis) on property ownership when requested by judicial authorities.
keeper-of-the-archives.bio: The bibliophylakes of Oxyrhynchus repeatedly investigated Chaeremon's property claims and confirmed Dionysia's rights. Their records were the documentary foundation of her legal victories.

agoranomos.title: Agoranomos
agoranomos.description: Public notary who executed and registered official contracts in the state archives.
agoranomos.duties: Witnessing, recording, and archiving contracts of sale, leases, and other legal instruments. The demosios chrematismos (official sale contract) that Dionysia received for her vineyard was produced through this office.
agoranomos.bio: The agoranomos's office produced the official sale contract that proved Dionysia's ownership of the vineyard against Sarapion's challenge.

phrontistes.title: Phrontistes
phrontistes.description: Village-level estate manager responsible for all agricultural production, labor management, financial accounting, and transport logistics.
phrontistes.duties: Managed vineyards and grain fields, supervised permanent and seasonal workers, rendered monthly financial and wheat accounts, coordinated wine and grain transport, hosted inspection visits, maintained irrigation infrastructure, tested wine quality by smell.
phrontistes.cost_burden: Salaried at 40 drachmas/month plus 1 artaba wheat
phrontistes.status_level: Mid-level manager, drawn from locally prominent rural families
phrontistes.bio: The phrontistes was the working edge of the Appianus estate's management hierarchy. From phrontis -- thought, care, anxiety -- the man who worried.

exegetes.title: Exegetes
exegetes.description: Municipal officer responsible for guardianship cases, religious functions, and other legal-administrative duties. The Oxyrhynchite exegetes also had jurisdiction over the Small Oasis.
exegetes.duties: Judicial and interpretive functions in Alexandrian civic government
exegetes.bio: Appianus held the title 'former exegetes' (apo exegetou), indicating he had served in this office and retained the status as an honorific.

bouleutes.title: Bouleutes
bouleutes.description: Councillor of the city of Alexandria
bouleutes.duties: Member of the city council (boule) with administrative and fiscal responsibilities
bouleutes.bio: Appianus held the title bouleutes of Alexandria.

diastoleus.title: Diastoleus
diastoleus.description: Agent responsible for distributing or allocating goods, particularly wine, between estate divisions and the city.
diastoleus.duties: Managed wine shipments, acknowledged receipt of goods, coordinated transport
diastoleus.bio: Maximos, son of Heroneinos, served as diastoleus, handling the flow of wine from the Fayum to Alexandria.

dekaprotos.title: Dekaprotos
dekaprotos.description: Local tax collectors personally liable for the tax quota of their district
dekaprotos.duties: Collected grain and cash taxes, measured out payments, reported arrears
dekaprotos.cost_burden: Personally liable for any shortfall in tax collection
dekaprotos.bio: The dekaprotoi at Theadelphia collected tax payments from Heroneinos on behalf of the Appianus estate.

oikonomos.title: Oikonomos
oikonomos.duties: Mid-level estate management and supply coordination
oikonomos.bio: Herakleides the oikonomos is mentioned in connection with reed supplies and grape harvest arrangements.

euschemon.title: Euschemon
euschemon.description: The honorable one -- circumlocution for the landowner
euschemon.bio: Used by subordinates to refer to Appianus without naming him directly. A conventional mark of deference in estate correspondence.

beneficiarius-praefecti-aegypti.title: Beneficiarius praefecti Aegypti
beneficiarius-praefecti-aegypti.description: Soldier of special rank attached to the praefect of Egypt, stationed at a fixed post (statio) in a provincial city. The beneficiarius performed police, judicial, and administrative functions: receiving civilian complaints about crimes, compelling individuals to appear before authorities, issuing orders to village officials (comarchs), and forwarding serious cases to the praefect's office. In late third-century Egypt, beneficiarii were a primary point of contact between ordinary people and the imperial legal system, particularly for petitions involving violence and property crimes.

epistrategos-of-the-heptanomia-and-the-s.title: Epistrategos of the Heptanomia and the Small Oasis
epistrategos-of-the-heptanomia-and-the-s.greek_term: ἐπιστράτηγος τῆς Ἑπτανομίας καὶ τῆς Μικρᾶς Ὀάσεως
epistrategos-of-the-heptanomia-and-the-s.description: Regional governor overseeing the seven nomes of Middle Egypt and the Small Oasis. Intermediate authority between the nome-level strategi and the praefect of Egypt.

prytanis.title: Prytanis
prytanis.description: Presiding officer of the city senate in Roman Egyptian metropoleis. In Oxyrhynchus, the prytanis oversaw council meetings, managed civic expenditures, and served as the senate's chief executive officer.

logistes-curator-civitatis.title: Logistes (Curator Civitatis)
logistes-curator-civitatis.description: Financial overseer introduced under Diocletian's administrative reforms to supervise civic finances in Egyptian cities.

comarch.title: Comarch
comarch.description: Village headman responsible for local administration, tax collection, and policing. Comarchs received and executed orders from beneficiarii and higher officials.

kyrios-guardian.title: Kyrios (Guardian)
kyrios-guardian.description: Male legal guardian required for women conducting legal transactions in Roman Egypt. Aloine's guardian was her nephew Komon son of Dioscorus, who assumed the role after her brother's death. The kyrios signed documents and lent legal standing, though the woman remained the principal party in the transaction.

archidicastes.title: Archidicastes
archidicastes.description: Chief judge in Alexandria, overseeing the chrematistae and other courts. The freedman Serenus petitioned the archidicastes Antoninus (also called Pudens) to enforce a defaulted loan in 178 CE, demonstrating that freedpersons had access to the highest levels of the judicial system.

porphyropoles-purple-seller.title: Porphyropoles (Purple-seller)
porphyropoles-purple-seller.description: Dealer in purple-dyed textiles and dyes. In late antique Egypt, the trade encompassed both dyeing and selling of finished purple goods. Workers could be independent sellers or hired employees (misthioi).
porphyropoles-purple-seller.duties: Dyeing textiles using plant-based or Murex-based purple dyes; selling finished goods; managing workshops with hired workers; supplying garments, veils, and fabrics to local and regional consumers.
porphyropoles-purple-seller.cost_burden: Required investment in raw materials (alkanet, lichen, mordants), workshop space, dye vats, fuel. Workshop owners paid workers' wages in grain.
porphyropoles-purple-seller.status_level: Artisan-merchant class. Modest but respectable. The family estate was described as small (elachiste hypostasis).

deacon-diakonos.title: Deacon (Diakonos)
deacon-diakonos.description: Lower-ranking member of the Christian clergy. In late antique Egyptian villages, deacons served as scribes and witnesses for legal transactions alongside their religious duties.
deacon-diakonos.duties: Religious duties; serving as scribes for illiterate parties; witnessing property sales, contracts, and settlements.
deacon-diakonos.status_level: Literate class in a largely illiterate village society.

priest-presbyteros.title: Priest (Presbyteros)
priest-presbyteros.description: Senior member of the Christian clergy in village churches. Priests witnessed legal transactions and provided moral authority.
priest-presbyteros.duties: Religious leadership; witnessing property sales, loans, and settlements.

symboilaiographos-notary.title: Symboilaiographos (Notary)
symboilaiographos-notary.description: Professional notary responsible for drafting formal legal documents. In This, a succession served: Ioulios (592 CE), Dioskoros (600 CE), and Paulos (605-616 CE).
symboilaiographos-notary.duties: Drafting sale deeds, inheritance divisions, loan documents, and employment contracts in proper legal form.
symboilaiographos-notary.status_level: Professional literate class with specialised legal knowledge.

oikodomos-buildermason.title: Oikodomos (Builder/Mason)
oikodomos-buildermason.description: Builder or mason who constructed and assessed buildings. Senouthes the oikodomos served as expert property appraiser in the 592 CE sale.
oikodomos-buildermason.duties: Construction and repair of houses; assessment of property values for sale transactions.
oikodomos-buildermason.status_level: Skilled artisan with expert knowledge of property values.

khoragos-chorus-leadersponsor.title: Khoragos (Chorus Leader/Sponsor)
khoragos-chorus-leadersponsor.description: In the Greek world, a khoragos (also transliterated choragos or choregos) was a wealthy citizen who financed and organized a chorus for competitive performance at a civic festival. The role was one of the most expensive forms of public service (leitourgia). In Apellis's tablet, the khoragoi are the primary targets of the curse, cursed 'for futility both in word and in deed.' The existence of multiple competing khoragoi at Gela implies organized choral festivals with formal competition.

boeotarch.title: Boeotarch
boeotarch.description: Elected military and political commander of the Boeotian League. Seven boeotarchs served at any given time. Epaminondas held the office repeatedly during the Theban hegemony. Pelopidas was notably NOT boeotarch at Leuctra but commanded the Sacred Band by special trust. The boeotarchs had to agree collectively before committing to battle.

captain-of-the-sacred-band.title: Captain of the Sacred Band
captain-of-the-sacred-band.description: Commander of the hieros lochos, the elite unit of 300 men quartered in the Cadmeia. The position carried enormous trust and prestige. Pelopidas held it from after Tegyra (375 BCE) until his death in 364 BCE. The commanders between Pelopidas' death and Chaeroneia are not recorded.

polemarch.title: Polemarch
polemarch.description: Senior magistrate at Thebes with military and judicial authority. Archias and Philip held the office as pro-Spartan appointees during the occupation of 382-379 BCE and were assassinated by Pelopidas' conspirators.

magical-practitioner-goes-magos.title: Magical Practitioner (goes / magos)
magical-practitioner-goes-magos.description: Professional ritual specialist who produced magical spells, amulets, and figurines for paying clients. Often drawn from displaced temple priests or itinerant scribes. Operated outside official religious institutions, using specialized knowledge of divine names, sacred languages, and ritual procedures. Roman law classified some of their activities as criminal.
magical-practitioner-goes-magos.duties: Consulting formularies to select appropriate procedures; inscribing spells on papyrus, lead, or other media; constructing figurines from wax or clay; preparing magical inks and substances; depositing finished spells in graves or other ritually potent locations; reciting voces magicae during ritual preparations.
magical-practitioner-goes-magos.cost_burden: Required access to writing materials (papyrus, lead, ink, myrrh), knowledge of multiple religious traditions (Egyptian, Greek, Jewish, Mesopotamian), and access to cemeteries for grave deposits. Fees charged to clients are poorly attested but appear to have been moderate.
magical-practitioner-goes-magos.status_level: Marginal. Possessed specialized knowledge equivalent to priestly training but lacked institutional support. Legally vulnerable to prosecution under Roman anti-magic legislation. Socially ambiguous -- simultaneously feared, despised, and indispensable.

idios-logos-special-account.title: Idios Logos (Special Account)
idios-logos-special-account.description: Roman administrative office in Egypt responsible for the management of revenues from irregular sources, including confiscations, fines, and taxes on marriages that violated regulations. The Gnomon of the Idios Logos codified the rules governing these revenues, including marriage and inheritance regulations.
idios-logos-special-account.duties: Enforcing marriage regulations; collecting fines for violations (age-disparate marriages, marriages between prohibited status groups); managing confiscated property of those who died without legal heirs.
idios-logos-special-account.status_level: High imperial administrative office, subordinate to the prefect of Egypt.

eunuch-eunoukhos.title: Eunuch (Eunoukhos)
eunuch-eunoukhos.description: A castrated male. The term literally means "bed-guardian" (from eune, bed, and ekhein, to keep), reflecting the original function of guarding the women's quarters. In practice, eunuchs served as personal attendants, chamberlains, treasurers, administrators, and military guardians. In the Persian Empire, they held positions of enormous trust and power. In the Roman Empire, they were subject to special legal disabilities including reduced inheritance rights.
eunuch-eunoukhos.duties: Guarding the royal bedchamber and women's quarters; personal service to rulers; treasury management (gazophylax); military service; diplomatic missions; administration. In the Persian court, eunuchs served in all positions "from the door-keepers up" (Xenophon).
eunuch-eunoukhos.status_level: Paradoxical: simultaneously the most despised and the most trusted category of servant. Socially contemptible (Xenophon) but politically indispensable. In Roman Egypt, registered and subject to property confiscation at death.

gallus-pl-galli.title: Gallus (pl. Galli)
gallus-pl-galli.description: A castrated priest of Cybele (Magna Mater) and her consort Attis. The Galli performed voluntary self-castration during the Dies Sanguinis (Day of Blood) on March 24, thereafter adopting women's clothing, cosmetics, and long hair. The name may derive from the river Gallus in Phrygia. Active from at least the 3rd century BCE through the 4th century CE.
gallus-pl-galli.duties: Serving at the temples of Cybele and Attis; participating in the annual March festivals (Canna intrat, Arbor intrat, Dies Sanguinis, Hilaria); ritual dancing, drumming, and self-flagellation; begging for alms; prophecy and divination.
gallus-pl-galli.cost_burden: The act of self-castration was irreversible and entailed permanent social marginality. Galli depended on the cult and on alms for sustenance. Roman law imposed inheritance penalties on eunuchs, including Galli.
gallus-pl-galli.status_level: Sacred yet abject. Priests of an officially recognised Roman cult but excluded from many civil rights. Roman citizens were forbidden from becoming Galli (though this prohibition was not always enforced). Objects of fascination and disgust in Roman literature.

enaree.title: Enaree
enaree.description: A category of Scythian people described by Herodotus as "hermaphrodites" who possessed the art of divination given to them by Aphrodite. The Scythians attributed the condition to a divine curse for sacrilege against the temple of Aphrodite at Ascalon. Modern scholars debate whether the Enarees were intersex individuals, transgender people, ritual specialists who adopted liminal gender roles, or some combination.
enaree.duties: Divination using lime-tree bark, which they braided and unbraided in their fingers. Prophetic functions within Scythian society.
enaree.status_level: Liminal. Marked as different by divine curse or gift, but socially functional as prophets and diviners.

gazophylax-treasurer.title: Gazophylax (Treasurer)
gazophylax-treasurer.description: Keeper of the royal treasury. In the Hellenistic kingdoms, this position was frequently held by eunuchs. When Demetrius Poliorcetes insulted Lysimachus by treating him as a eunuch, the sting came from the association of eunuchs with the treasurer's role. The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8 served a comparable function under Queen Candace.
gazophylax-treasurer.duties: Management of royal treasure, financial administration, disbursement of funds.
gazophylax-treasurer.status_level: High responsibility but socially ambiguous due to association with eunuch status.

idios-logos-administrator-of-special-acc.title: Idios Logos (Administrator of Special Accounts)
idios-logos-administrator-of-special-acc.description: The Roman official in Egypt responsible for administering properties, inheritances, and revenues that fell to the state through legal forfeit, intestacy, or penalty. The Gnomon of the Idios Logos (BGU 5.1210) is the administrative manual for this office, containing regulations on inheritance, marriage, priestly conduct, and the property of eunuchs.
idios-logos-administrator-of-special-acc.duties: Enforcing inheritance laws; confiscating properties of those who died intestate or violated marriage regulations; registering eunuchs and administering their estates; managing the fiscus.
idios-logos-administrator-of-special-acc.status_level: Senior provincial administrator, answering to the Roman prefect of Egypt.

amulet-practitioner.title: Amulet Practitioner
amulet-practitioner.description: Producer of protective and healing amulets. In antiquity, practitioners ranged from itinerant specialists carrying "charms in boxes" (Shepherd of Hermas) to temple priests, Christian clergy, and Jewish scribes. They drew from professional handbooks containing tested formulae with blank spaces for client names.
amulet-practitioner.duties: Consulting with clients about their ailments; selecting appropriate formulae and divine names; inscribing amulets on papyrus, parchment, metal lamellae, or gemstones; folding or preparing amulets for wearing; possibly performing accompanying rituals or incantations.
amulet-practitioner.cost_burden: Materials ranged from inexpensive (papyrus strips) to costly (gold lamellae, engraved gemstones). Professional fees are poorly attested but the accessibility of the cheapest amulets suggests they served all social classes.
amulet-practitioner.status_level: Ambiguous. Condemned by both Hippocratic physicians and Christian authorities, yet employed by all levels of society. Some practitioners were literate professionals with knowledge of medicine, theology, and multiple languages.

hippocratic-physician.title: Hippocratic Physician
hippocratic-physician.description: Practitioner of rational medicine in the tradition of the Hippocratic Corpus. Diagnosed and treated diseases through observation, dietary regimen, and physical interventions rather than religious ritual.
hippocratic-physician.duties: Observing symptoms and disease patterns; prescribing dietary changes and regimens; prognosticating outcomes; maintaining case records (as in the Epidemics).
hippocratic-physician.cost_burden: Professional consultations were expensive relative to amulets. Physicians served primarily the wealthy and urban populations.
hippocratic-physician.status_level: High social prestige in theory, but in practice competed with magical healers for patients across all social classes.

temple-priest-hiereus-stolistes.title: Temple Priest (hiereus / stolistes)
temple-priest-hiereus-stolistes.description: Priest serving at an Egyptian temple. In the Fayum, priests of the crocodile god Soknopaios (and related deities like Soxis Pnepheros) administered oracle services alongside their liturgical duties. The stolistes was specifically responsible for the cult statue's vestments.
temple-priest-hiereus-stolistes.duties: Receiving oracle questions from petitioners; administering the oracle procedure (presenting tickets to the god, returning the selected answer); maintaining the temple; performing daily rituals; serving as local administrators and tax intermediaries.
temple-priest-hiereus-stolistes.cost_burden: Priests held temple lands and derived income from endowments and temple services. Oracle consultation likely required a fee or offering.
temple-priest-hiereus-stolistes.status_level: Local elite. Priests at Soknopaiou Nesos combined religious authority with economic substance.

oracle-book-operator.title: Oracle Book Operator
oracle-book-operator.description: Literate individual who operated a lot oracle book (such as the Sortes Astrampsychi) for clients, generating divine answers through a standardised random procedure involving dice or numbered tickets.
oracle-book-operator.duties: Identifying the client's question from the standard list; performing the random operation (dice throw, lot drawing); looking up and delivering the corresponding answer from the book.
oracle-book-operator.cost_burden: Required literacy, a copy of the oracle book, and a randomising device (dice, knucklebones, or numbered tokens).
oracle-book-operator.status_level: Variable. Could be a temple priest, an itinerant specialist, or any literate person with access to the text. The decentralised nature of the practice worried church authorities.

cosmetes.title: cosmetes
cosmetes.description: Municipal official responsible for the gymnasium, ephebic training, festivals, and associated civic display.
cosmetes.duties: Supervised youth training, processions, athletic events, and parts of the city's ceremonial calendar.
cosmetes.cost_burden: Heavy. The holder often had to spend personal funds to sustain the office.
cosmetes.status_level: Prestigious but financially dangerous liturgical office.

granary-clerk.title: granary clerk
granary-clerk.description: Local fiscal official responsible for entering grain deliveries and deficits into granary books.
granary-clerk.duties: Measured wheat, recorded names, amounts, quarters, and intermediaries in official registers.
granary-clerk.status_level: Administrative functionary within the local fiscal apparatus.

tax-collector.title: tax collector
tax-collector.description: Collector handling market dues, bath taxes, guard-post levies, and other municipal obligations.
tax-collector.duties: Gathered payments, issued ostraca, and routed obligations into district accounts.

record-office-clerk-registrar.title: Record-office clerk / registrar
record-office-clerk-registrar.description: Local scribal official responsible for registering contracts, divorce deeds, receipts, and other private legal instruments.
record-office-clerk-registrar.duties: Drafted and archived marriage contracts, divorce deeds, receipts, cessions, and acknowledgements, giving them public legal force.
record-office-clerk-registrar.status_level: Local administrative office central to private legal life in villages such as Tebtunis.

strategos.title: Strategos
strategos.description: Senior district official in Roman Egypt who handled administrative and judicial business, including disputes that could escalate beyond village record offices.
strategos.duties: Oversaw district administration, received petitions, supervised records, and heard or transmitted disputes requiring official action.
strategos.status_level: High regional office within the nome administration.

record-office-registrar.title: Record-office / registrar
record-office-registrar.description: Local office where contracts, property declarations, and other legal instruments were filed and validated.
record-office-registrar.duties: Registered declarations of ownership, contracts, receipts, and other private legal acts that made households legible to the state.
record-office-registrar.status_level: Local administrative institution central to documentary survival.

wet-nurse.title: Wet nurse
wet-nurse.description: Woman contracted to nurse and care for another household's infant, often under detailed wage and conduct restrictions.
wet-nurse.duties: Fed the child with her own milk, housed and watched the infant, and presented the child for inspection according to contract terms.
wet-nurse.cost_burden: Bore intense bodily and domestic obligations, including restrictions on sex, pregnancy, and the nursing of other children.
wet-nurse.status_level: Domestic labor role, usually subordinate but capable of becoming legally formalized through contract.

stolistes.title: Stolistes
stolistes.description: Priest charged with the vestments and dressing of a cult image.
stolistes.duties: Managed sacred clothing, supervised image care, and helped maintain the visible dignity of cult.
stolistes.cost_burden: Depended on recurring textile supply, repair, storage, and ritual replacement.
stolistes.status_level: Important priestly office in Egyptian temple hierarchy.

lamplighter.title: Lamplighter
lamplighter.description: Temple worker responsible for daily lamp service in shrines.
lamplighter.duties: Maintained lamps, ensured correct oil supply, and could be bound by oath and property surety for failure.
lamplighter.cost_burden: Required steady access to oil and therefore tied ritual continuity to fuel logistics.
lamplighter.status_level: Subordinate but indispensable sacred service role.

prophet.title: Prophet
prophet.description: Senior temple office named in fiscal accounts alongside other cult personnel.
prophet.duties: Participated in ritual leadership and temple administration.
prophet.cost_burden: Appears in accounts as an office carrying direct fiscal weight.

temple-steward.title: Temple Steward
temple-steward.description: Manager of sacred property, supplies, and operating resources.
temple-steward.duties: Oversaw assets such as cattle, rents, storehouses, deliveries, and perhaps parts of temple payroll or provisioning.
temple-steward.status_level: Essential administrative role linking cult to the wider economy.

logistes.title: Logistes
logistes.description: Financial and administrative official who could issue public notices connected to performance.
logistes.duties: Managed or supervised fiscal and administrative business at city level.

prostates.title: Prostates
prostates.description: Village president or local official; in this dossier, one of the figures who helps procure entertainment.
prostates.duties: Represented village interests and handled local administrative coordination.

public-physician.title: Public physician
public-physician.description: A doctor recognized by civic authority and liable to be summoned for inspections and reports.
public-physician.duties: Examined bodies, certified illness, and submitted written reports to magistrates and officials.
public-physician.cost_burden: Could be salaried from designated fiscal streams or public funds.

chief-physician.title: Chief physician
chief-physician.description: Senior medical title appearing in the temple dossier of Athenagoras.
chief-physician.duties: Supervised medical or medico-administrative work within an institutional setting.

horse-doctor.title: Horse doctor
horse-doctor.description: Specialized veterinary counterpart to the ordinary physician, visible in ration accounts.
horse-doctor.duties: Treated horses and appears in documentary rations beside ordinary physicians.

perfume-seller.title: Perfume seller
perfume-seller.description: Retail specialist visible in letters and commercial records as a perfume seller.
perfume-seller.duties: Sold perfumed goods, handled customers, and could appear in leases and correspondence by title.

perfumer.title: Perfumer
perfumer.description: Occupational world marked by terms connected to perfumery production.
perfumer.duties: Mixed, prepared, or manufactured scented substances for sale or distribution.

barber.title: Barber
barber.description: Trade visible through shops, payments, and equipment requests.
barber.duties: Cut hair, shaved clients, and worked through shops and portable equipment.

bath-worker.title: Bath worker
bath-worker.description: Profession implied indirectly by the repeated expense, heating, and fuel dossier around bathhouses.
bath-worker.duties: Helped maintain bath operation through heating, provisioning, and routine upkeep.
bath-worker.cost_burden: Depended on fuel, buildings, and steady operating supplies.
bath-worker.status_level: Service role attached to bath infrastructure.

prefect-of-egypt.title: Prefect of Egypt
prefect-of-egypt.description: Highest provincial official to whom the dispute was escalated.
prefect-of-egypt.duties: Governed Roman Egypt and issued decisions or decrees affecting provincial legal practice.
